chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the Committee on Criminal Justice hearing, asks phones be silenced, and reminds attendees not to approach the dais.
chapters.title: Council staff opens hearing and announces housekeeping rules

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to the Committee on Criminal Justice.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please place your phone on silent or vibrate mode.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At any time during the hearing, do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers welcomes everyone to the oversight hearing on improving court operations to reduce the jail population, noting Judge Zayas's participation and the importance of case processing speed for closing Rikers Island.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers introduces hearing on improving court operations to reduce jail population

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Selvena Brooks-Powers, Chair of the Committee on Criminal Justice.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I want to welcome everyone to today's hearing on improving court operations to reduce the jail population.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: At the outset, I would like to extend my gratitude to Judge Zayas on his superb

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: staff and team and for participating in today's conversation.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Judge Zayas has demonstrated his commitment to changing the status quo and to creating a legal system that promotes community safety by reducing reliance on incarceration.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I am joined by committee members.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers highlights that over 80% of city jail detainees are pretrial, and references Kalief Browder's story to underscore the human consequences of prolonged pretrial detention and delayed court proceedings.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers on pretrial detention and the human cost of case delays

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Brewer and Murano.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The pace at which cases move throughout courts is a critical factor in determining how New York City will achieve its legal mandate to close Rikers Island and transition to borough-based jails.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: At present, more than 80% of the people in city jails are pretrial detainees, individuals who have not been convicted but are held while the cases are pending.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: When those cases are delayed, people remain in custody.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Longer than necessary, the jail population is inflated, and questions are raised as to whether the planned capacity of the borough-based jails will be sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As context for this hearing, it is important to recognize the real-world consequences of prolonged pretrial detention in our city jails.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The experience of individuals like Kalief Browder, who spent years at Rikers Island without a conviction, while his case was repeatedly delayed, underscores the human cost of a system where case

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: processing breaks down.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: His stories are a reminder that delays in court proceedings are not abstract administrative problems, but have profound and lasting consequences.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers discusses how delayed justice erodes public confidence, especially in communities with frequent minor offense consequences, leaving everyone less safe.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers on impact of delays on victims and community confidence

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Those consequences are felt not only by people unnecessarily detained, but also by crime victims and their loved ones.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: When someone is victimized,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: especially by an act of violence, we should expect swift accountability.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: When justice is delayed, especially in communities where immediate consequences for minor offenses are frequent and highly visible, people rightly lose confidence in the criminal justice system, the criminal legal system.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As a result, we are all left less safe.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers details the hearing's focus areas: discovery compliance, court scheduling, evidence production, interagency coordination between OCA, NYPD, and MOCJ, and testimony from legal service providers and community organizations.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers outlines hearing topics: discovery, scheduling, interagency coordination

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Today's hearing will examine the operational factors that contribute to case delays, including discovery compliance, court scheduling practices,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: evidence production, and interagency coordination between the Office of Court Administration, the NYPD, and the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers explains the hearing will examine how discovery obligations from 2019 reforms and subsequent amendments affect trial readiness and case resolution timelines.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers on discovery reforms and trial readiness

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Also, the committee will hear testimony from legal service providers, community organizations, and other stakeholders about how these systems function in practice and where breakdowns continue to occur.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We will focus in particular on how discovery obligations are being implemented following the initial 2019

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: statutory reforms and subsequent amendments, and how those requirements are affecting trial readiness and case resolution timelines.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We will also examine how court operations, such as the timing of hearings, the resolution of discovery disputes, and the availability of court resources shape the length of pretrial.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers thanks senior counsel Jeremy Whiteman on his final hearing, policy analyst Chad Benjamin, financial analyst Julian Martin on his last hearing, communications director Kiera Powell, and chief of staff Renee Taylor.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers thanks committee staff including departing members

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: pre-trial

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I would like to thank my staff and committee staff for their hard work.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Jeremy Whiteman, senior counsel to the committee, who is serving in his very final hearing with the city council.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I just want to thank you for the work that you've provided to the committee all of these years.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Senior counsel to the committee.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Also, we have Chad Benjamin, policy analyst.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Casey Lageski.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Financial analyst Julian Martin, my deputy chief of staff, who also is serving in his last hearing before departing to go on to his next chapter.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And we'd like to thank Julian for all of his amazing work, both on the Committee on Criminal Justice and the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: My communications director, Kiera Powell, and of course my chief of staff, Renee Taylor.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I look forward to a productive discussion about how we can strengthen court operations to enhance fairness and accountability across the board.

chapters.summary: Council staff swears in Judge Joseph A. Zayas, who affirms to tell the truth before the committee.
chapters.title: Judge Joseph Zayas sworn in before committee testimony

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So now we'll call Judge Zayas up to the dais, please.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And council will sway you in.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Judge Ice, do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing short of the truth before this committee and respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Noting for the record, it was answered affirmatively.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Now, my light is red here.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Should it be green?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: No, you're good.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Judge Zayas opens by emphasizing his deep commitment to efficient case management, drawing on his experience as a trial and administrative judge, and explains why serious criminal cases cannot routinely languish in courts.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on importance of efficient criminal case processing

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: May I begin?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: All right, good morning, Chair Brooks-Powers.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Thank you and the other members of this committee for organizing today's hearing on a truly important subject, the processing of criminal cases in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: As you said in your opening remarks, which I really appreciated, the efficient management of criminal cases is something that I care deeply, deeply about, having served for many years as a trial judge and an administrative judge.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Now, as the chief administrative judge of overseeing the operations of all of New York State's

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: trial courts, one of my highest priorities is the implementation of policies and procedures that help minimize the delays that often impede the swift resolution of criminal cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Let me start by explaining why this issue is so important.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: For our criminal justice system to be effective and credible, serious criminal cases cannot routinely languish in the courts.

chapters.summary: Zayas highlights the importance of efficiency for problem-solving courts providing diversion for substance use, mental health, human trafficking victims, adolescents, veterans, and others, noting faster engagement improves intervention effectiveness.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on problem-solving courts and diversion opportunities

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Allowing criminal cases to proceed at a pace that lacks an appropriate sense of urgency results in a loss of confidence in the court system's ability to do its job.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: When, for example, there is a murder in one of our communities,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: and someone is arrested, it should not take two or three years for the case to be resolved.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Witnesses, affected neighborhoods, and the families of victims deserve better.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So too do the individuals who are charged.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Efficiency is not just

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: essential for cases that are resolved in the traditional ways, either with a bail bargain, a plea bargain, I'm sorry, or a trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It is also critically important to the efficacy of our problem-solving courts, which provide diversion opportunities for individuals with substance use disorders, mental health issues, victims of

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: human trafficking, adolescents, and emerging adults, veterans, and others.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It should go without saying that the faster we engage these individuals with the services and treatments they need, the more likely these interventions are able to be effective.

chapters.summary: Zayas outlines court system efforts to address case delays and suggests ways other justice stakeholders must step up to achieve the goal of reducing the jail population to around 4,000 for borough-based jails.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on court system initiatives and need for stakeholder cooperation

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: This morning, I'm excited to highlight all of the things the court system is doing to address these case delays.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But just as importantly, I'm going to suggest several ways in which other justice system

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Stakeholders must step up if we're going to achieve the goal that I know is so important to this council, reducing the jail population to around 4,000 so that it can be accommodated by the borough-based jails.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The bottom line is this.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Improving case processing.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: efficiency in high-volume courts is not easy.

chapters.summary: Zayas details factors outside court control that delay cases: prosecution discovery timelines, defense review obligations, competency restoration waits, and private defense attorneys managing excessive caseloads without hiring associates.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on factors beyond court control causing case delays

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It truly requires an all-hands-on-the-deck approach.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The simplest reason is that while we would like to believe that judges have the authority to make criminal cases proceed at whatever pace they choose, in actuality,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There are many factors that affect case processing that are beyond the court's control.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: To list just a few, cases get delayed when it takes the prosecution months upon months to provide all of the discovery that the defense is entitled to under New York's robust discovery law.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Which sometimes results from the lack of effective coordination between prosecutors and the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Cases get delayed because defense attorneys have a constitutional obligation to carefully review the discovery they receive.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Hundreds or even thousands of pages of police reports and medical records, along with the hours of body camera footage recorded by the officers who responded to the scene.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Cases get delayed when a defendant who has been found unfit to proceed must wait months.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Before they can begin receiving competency restoration services at an OMH hospital.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And perhaps most frustrating of all, cases are delayed when private defense attorneys have dozens of paying clients from across the city.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: far more than a solo practitioner can reasonably manage, but choose not to hire associates to handle their caseload.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: These are not excuses.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: They are facts.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And if we're going to improve the case processing in a long-term, sustainable way, we need to be realistic about the myriad challenges.

chapters.summary: Zayas describes new scheduling orders in NYC Supreme Courts setting deadlines for discovery production and defense issues, with court attorney-led conferences to resolve disputes efficiently, piloted in Brooklyn with nearly 100% increase in dispositions.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on scheduling orders and proactive case management

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So what is the court system doing about case processing?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: For starters, we are urging judges and court attorneys to take a more proactive approach to case management and giving them the tools and resources to do so effectively.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Throughout New York City's Supreme Courts, where indicted felonies are handled, judges are now issuing scheduling orders that set clear timelines for getting through the case's preliminary stages.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: These orders establish deadlines for the production of discovery by the prosecution, as well as for the defense, to raise issues with the people's discovery compliance.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And they mandate court attorney-led conferences with the parties which provide a forum for discovery disputes to be resolved efficiently, hopefully without the need for time-consuming motion practice.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Importantly, these conferences also encourage early discussions about case resolutions.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In Brooklyn, where we first began issuing these orders at the end of 2024, an executive assistant district attorney

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: attends every conference, and makes a plea offer, which helped contribute in 2025 to a nearly 100% increase in the number of dispositions in the two court parts that were part of the pilot.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Perhaps this scheduling order initiative doesn't sound that groundbreaking, but it is.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: When I was a public defender for nine years, and also when I was a judge,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It was basically unheard of for judges in New York City's criminal courts to issue scheduling orders.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But for reasons that almost go without saying, it's best practices for judges at the beginning of every case to set clear scheduling expectations

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: so that the parties understand that the case is going to proceed on the court's timeline, not theirs.

chapters.summary: Zayas explains dedicated court attorneys for homicides, felony sex crimes, and gang prosecutions, plus a 30% increase in problem-solving court staff statewide, serving over 31,000 New Yorkers last year.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on infrastructure for complex cases and problem-solving courts

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We've also set up infrastructure in each borough to ensure that homicides, felony sex crimes, multiple defendant gang prosecutions, and other complex matters, which tend to be difficult to resolve,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: move towards this position.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There are many reasons these cases get delayed.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The discovery can be voluminous, plea bargaining is more fraught, and scheduling is complicated because even in New York City, we have too few lawyers on both sides handling the most serious criminal cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: This is why we've hired experienced court attorneys in each of our Supreme Courts to aggressively

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: conference these cases, identify barriers to progress, and develop firm plans for moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In addition, over the past two years, we have significantly ramped up the number of non-judicial personnel serving in our problem-solving courts statewide.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Statewide, we've added nearly 100 project directors, case managers, and resource coordinators,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: amounting to a 30% increase in programmatic staff, which has allowed us to reduce caseloads in some of our busiest courts, particularly in New York City, and open new ones.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In fact, last year, problem-solving courts in the city's Supreme Courts served over 31...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: 100 New Yorkers, nearly 1,000 more than we served in 2022.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And credit for that must be shared with our district attorneys and our defense bar, who have demonstrated a laudable commitment to alternative forms of accountability in criminal cases.

chapters.summary: Zayas describes dedicated positions coordinating with DOC on defendant production, regular meetings with City Hall, a passed state bill on sentencing flexibility, and rollout of electronic filing in Supreme Courts.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on interagency coordination and legislative successes

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We are indeed focused on improving case

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: processing efficiency from every conceivable angle.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We have established dedicated positions in each borough to coordinate with the New York City Department of Corrections on issues related to the production of defendants that can stall cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And we're now meeting regularly with representatives from City Hall on a range of issues of mutual concern with case processing.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: at the very top of the list.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And of course, Chair, we're happy to meet more regularly with you and members of this committee as well.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We were also successful at getting the state legislature this year to pass one of our program bills, which we hope the governor will sign, that will give judges the authority, with the party's consent,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: to dispense with recidivist sentencing provisions and plea bargaining restrictions that can make the resolution of certain cases very difficult.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Finally, over the last year, we've begun to roll out electronic filing in the city's Supreme Courts.

chapters.summary: Zayas reports every borough had more dispositions in 2025 than 2024, with Brooklyn up 15% and citywide up 10%; 2026 dispositions up 11% with a 125% clearance rate, and old incarcerated cases below 500 for the first time since 2020.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on positive disposition trends and clearance rates

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm pleased to report that the early results of these efforts are quite promising.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Every borough in New York City had more dispositions last year than it did in 2024.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Brooklyn, where we first piloted this work in late 2024, saw a substantial 15% increase, and citywide dispositions increased by 10%.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: That positive trend is continuing.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So far this year, dispositions in our Supreme Courts are up 11%.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And we are resolving cases significantly faster than new filings are coming in, which is important.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: This is our year-to-date clearance rate, which reflects the percentage of cases that have been disposed compared to how many were filed, which is now 125%.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Another positive data point, at the end of 2021, as we began to emerge from the pandemic, we had 781 cases that were over two years old with incarcerated defendants.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Earlier this year, that number dipped below 500 for the first time since 2020.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We can do better, though.

chapters.summary: Zayas discusses how NYC's 20-day discovery deadline for incarcerated defendants remains an aspiration, noting cases rarely resolve before discovery is complete and urging a serious conversation about when compliance will become the norm.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on discovery compliance challenges and 20-day deadline

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: As I said, we need the help of other justice system stakeholders to continue to move the needle.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Let me start with discovery.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: New York's discovery statute says that in cases in which the defendant is incarcerated, discovery must be provided within 20 days of arraignment.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In New York City, this deadline largely remains an aspiration.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm not unsympathetic to the fact that our discovery laws are extremely onerous.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And I know that the city's district attorneys take their discovery obligations seriously.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But I hope that this morning we can have a serious conversation about when compliance with the discovery deadlines that were a key component of the 2020 criminal justice reforms will become the norm.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Because the fact of the matter is this, cases rarely resolve before discovery is complete.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If discovery were routinely produced in 20 or 25 days rather than 60 or 90 days,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: cases would be resolved much faster.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Put simply, if one of the central selling points of discovery reform, namely that earlier, broader discovery of case information will facilitate earlier case resolutions,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: is going to be realized, we need to actually have earlier disclosure.

chapters.summary: Zayas notes 98% of criminal convictions result from guilty pleas, yet prosecutors often don't make plea offers timely, and calls for district attorneys to commit to earlier plea bargaining in most cases.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on plea bargaining and prosecutorial practices

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: This brings me to the next point.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In New York, 98% of criminal convictions are the result of guilty pleas, not trials.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Whatever the downsides of this state of affairs, the fact is that a very small percentage of criminal cases are tried.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Nevertheless, in many cases in New York City, including many relatively straightforward cases, prosecutors do not make plea offers in a timely manner.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: While there may be strategic reasons for the defense to tolerate or even encourage delay, that is almost never true for prosecutors.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: From the perspective of prosecutors, criminal cases typically do not get better with age.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But judges do not control plea bargaining.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Prosecutors do.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So another issue that I hope will be discussed today is whether our district attorneys will commit to engaging in plea bargaining earlier in most cases.

chapters.summary: Zayas highlights a NYC Bar Association report finding the Assigned Counsel Plan is understaffed and under-resourced, with MOCJ failing to access over $50 million allocated since 2018, and urges the new mayoral administration to address this.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on assigned counsel plan and $50 million funding gap

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The defense bar, too, is essential to improving case processing efficiency.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Private attorneys assigned as counsel to represent criminal.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: defendants pursuant to county law 18B, basically we're talking about 18B attorneys, consistently litigate around 50% of the cases that go to trial in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But as a recent report from the New York City Bar Association highlighted, the New York City assigned counsel plan

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: administration is understaffed and under-resourced and is thus unable to provide panel attorneys with the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In fact, according to the Bar Association's report, since 2018, the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice has failed to access over $50 million

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: that Indigent Legal Services has allocated to the New York City Assigned Counsel Plan under two separate contracts.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I hope and I expect that the new mayoral administration will work quickly to address these issues.

chapters.summary: Zayas encourages public defenders to support case processing initiatives and calls on the city to adequately fund programs and services for problem-solving court referrals, emphasizing expansion requires corresponding service provider capacity.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on public defender cooperation and city funding for services

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I also want to encourage the public defender organizations to give fair consideration to case processing initiatives when we propose them.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Obviously, we need their cooperation as well to make progress in this area.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Finally, the city can help too.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: by, among other things, adequately funding the programs and services to which our problem-solving courts refer defendants.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We are committed to continuing to expand these courts, but that expansion will only be impactful if there is a

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: corresponding increase in service provider capacity.

chapters.summary: Zayas expresses optimism about continued improvements, citing commitment from the court system, new mayoral administration, city council, five district attorneys, and defense bar, and offers to answer questions.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas on optimism for collaborative case processing improvements

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Despite the challenges I've just laid out, I am overly optimistic that we can continue to make significant improvements to case processing efficiency in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The court system

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: is deeply committed to this work.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We now have a mayoral administration that shares this commitment and a city council that desperately wants to see the jails on Rikers Island closed.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We're also fortunate at this moment to have five forward-thinking district attorneys and a sophisticated

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: passionate defense bar.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: For all of these reasons, I believe the time is ripe for a renewed commitment by all stakeholders to efficient case processing that does not sacrifice fairness to either prosecutors or defendants.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Working together, I honestly believe that we can do this.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And I'm happy to answer any questions that the chair or other committee members have about this topic.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers reads remarks from Councilmember Stevens, chair of the Committee on Children and Youth, emphasizing that reducing incarceration must include young people and addressing the youth justice system continuum from juvenile facilities to adult detention.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers reads remarks from Councilmember Althea Stevens on youth justice

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Judge.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Salam.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And before I start questioning, I will read brief remarks from Councilmember Althea Stevens, who serves as the chair for the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As chair of the Committee on Children and Youth, I want to emphasize that any conversation

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: about reducing incarceration must include young people.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Following the recent incident at Horizon Juvenile Center, it is clear that we cannot examine challenges within our justice system in pieces.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We must look at the entire continuum from youth facilities to adult detention and understand how each part impacts the other.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If we focus on adult facilities such as Rikers Island without addressing what is happening within our youth justice system, we will not solve the problem to its entirety.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The young people in our care today are the adults who will enter our criminal legal system tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If we fail to intervene with meaningful support, effective programming, timely court processing, and opportunities for rehabilitation.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Reducing the jail population is not just about moving cases faster.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It's about ensuring that every person, especially every young person, has access to justice services and the resources needed to prevent deeper involvement in the criminal justice system.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If we do not invest in youth now, we risk seeing the numbers at Rikers grow again in the years ahead.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Today's hearing is an opportunity to examine how our courts can operate more efficiently while ensuring fairness, accountability, and better outcomes for New Yorkers of all ages.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I look forward to having meaningful conversations.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: to develop a plan to reduce the census population in the juvenile detention center in the near future.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And now I'll pivot to questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just one moment, please.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: No problem.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Technical difficulties over here.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks whether discovery materials are being served to defense counsel within statutory timeframes. Zayas says clearly they are not, with compliance taking roughly twice the required time.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions Judge Zayas on discovery compliance with state law timeframes

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But you mentioned that judges are now issuing scheduling orders to set deadlines and prioritize the discovery process.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In general, would you say discovery materials are being served to the defense counsel in accordance with the timeframes established in state law?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I think it's very clear that they are not.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: As I indicated, the discovery statute calls for really accelerated disclosure.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I think it's 20 days for defendants who are being detained, 35 days for defendants who are not.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And we'll get you the numbers on each of those.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We do keep track of the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: the times in which they file a certificate of compliance with discovery.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And I don't want to be critical of my partners

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: in government, the district attorney's office, but I think it's clear, and I think they would admit that they are not complying with the letter of the law.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In the discovery statute.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So it's maybe twice the time is probably more like it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Understood.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers requests county-by-county data on discovery deadline adherence. Zayas discusses the onerous nature of discovery law, coordination challenges with NYPD, and agrees to provide the breakdown.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks for county-by-county discovery deadline breakdown

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And can you provide a county-by-county breakdown on adherence to the discovery deadlines?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Are variations between counties, what do you think the root cause could be?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Like, is it a resource thing, or is it that they just have a higher concentration?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I want to try to stay in my lane here in terms of my neutral role, but I think that's a very good question to ask them.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But I will say, look, the discovery statute is required.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's been six years now, so you think about this.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The discovery reform statute took effect six years ago.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But it does impose...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Extremely onerous burdens on the district attorney's office.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And as I said in my opening statement, I do think that they are working hard to take it seriously.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But the sheer voluminous nature of the discovery and maybe the need for some technological solution to make it happen, which I know that there were efforts at that,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm sure would help.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Maybe some coordination with the NYPD in terms of collaboration with them to get that discovery directly to the DA's office so that they can categorize it.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Because what the DA's office needs to do is present it to the defense in a way that it makes it

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: easy to use by the defense so that they're not just dumped on with a thousand pages, but that it's categorized by witness, by body cam, you know, the person whose body cam it was.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So, but I don't think there's any question that they have been, they've been having a really hard time complying with the strict deadlines that have been imposed by the statute.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: No, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And just kind of going back to the beginning of the question, which doesn't necessarily have to opine on why they are, because to your point, we are going to follow up with them.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The district attorneys all have submitted testimony because they have a conference today, and as a result, they were unable to be here.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But I would like to know if you would be able to bring...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Provide a breakdown by county.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In terms of the adherence to the guidelines.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In terms of?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The adherence to the discovery deadlines?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: County by county?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We do keep track of that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So I believe, I'm pretty sure we will be able to give you that information.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So do you have it now or?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I have it somewhere.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It might take me a minute to find it in my paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We'll move on, but if you could get into it, that would be great.

chapters.summary: Zayas shares certificate of compliance timing data: Bronx 130 days average, Kings 74 days, New York 145 days, Queens 60 days, Richmond 46 days, noting these exclude complex homicide cases.
chapters.title: Judge Zayas provides county-by-county discovery compliance data

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Oh, I think I do have it here.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So these are days that it takes them to file the certificate of compliance.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so in the Bronx, the average day is 130 days, 112 median days.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In Kings County, it's 74 days.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: average days, 56 median days.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: New York County, it's 145 days and 105 median days.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In Queens, which is where I was the administrative judge and chair, I know you're from Queens too,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: In terms of that, they are probably doing the best.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's average 60 days and 44 median days.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But nothing different.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: What's that?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I expect nothing different at the best from Queens.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And then in Richmond, it's 46 average days and 28 median days.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So, and, you know, that doesn't include the really more complex cases such as homicides of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So these are just your other serious felonies, but not homicides.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that, Judge.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers raises defense provider concerns about prosecutors filing certificates of compliance without turning over all materials, then serving supplemental discovery later. Zayas acknowledges this as problematic and encourages earlier piecemeal disclosure.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions supplemental discovery and certificate of compliance issues

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: One issue raised by defense providers is that after the 2025 amendments to the discovery laws, they've seen an increase in circumstances where the prosecution files

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: a certificate of compliance with discovery obligations months into the life of a case, but without

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: actually turning over all of the relevant material and then relying on their ability to serve supplemental discovery at a later date.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Is this something you've also identified as problematic and a cause of case delays?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, I'm not trying to come to the defense of the prosecutors in this case, but given sometimes prosecutors don't come across this information until later on.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So I think the defense is right about that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There are it's sort of a continuing process.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And we've been encouraging the district attorney's offices to actually start giving over discovery that they have right away.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Like, forget about the deadlines.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If you have discovery that you think.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: That is clearly discoverable, why not just start providing it to them without certifying that you've

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: completed the, because that gives the defense sort of a head start and they're not dumped on at the, I don't use that word derogatory,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's not a criticism, but the statute gives a date, and then it could be hundreds of documents, maybe thousands of documents that they're given at the last minute.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So if there could be a piecemeal approach by it being given over earlier, I don't really– we've been encouraging that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Sometimes documents take a little longer and then a supplemental disclosure has to happen.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And, of course, that causes delays.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The question is whether the supplemental documents that were later turned over, whether there was good cause or good reason why they didn't have it at the time that it was required.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I understand.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks how often judges impose sanctions for late discovery. Zayas is unsure if OCA tracks this but offers to provide data if available.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks about sanctions for late-produced discovery

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And how often are judges imposing sanctions for late produced discovery?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm not sure we're keeping track of that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If we are, we will send that to you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I just want to acknowledge we've been joined by Councilmember Caban.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers cites National Center for State Courts standards (75% of felonies disposed within 90 days) and asks how NYC compares. Zayas says NYC clearly does not meet these, noting NYC's vastly different caseloads and robust defense bar.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions NYC comparison to national case disposition standards

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So according to standards set by National Center for State Courts, 75% of felony cases should reach disposition within 90 days.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: 90% should reach disposition within 180 days and 98% within 365 days.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How does New York City compare to these national standards?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So we could get to those numbers as well.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We clearly do not.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Maybe in the first part of that, there's more compliance.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But when you're talking about the more serious cases, you know, New York is different in all sorts of ways from other small states.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, our caseloads are, you know,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: 20, 30, 40, 50 times more than the caseloads that they might have in these small jurisdictions in Utah.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so that's what they're comparing it to.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And New York also has, you know, a very robust defense bar.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so I'm not, I don't think that we are aspiring to meet those

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: center for court, the state center for court's timeline because New York is so different.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks how OCA works to change judge behavior given judicial independence. Zayas describes strongly encouraging proactive case management, hiring law clerks for discovery dispute conferences, and devoting resources to resolve issues.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks how OCA changes judicial behavior on case processing

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Given that judges are independent actors, how is OCA working to change behavior in case processing?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So, yeah, so we are strongly encouraging our judges to be more proactive in resolving discovery disputes.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So what was happening early on was, you know, we had these old cases, and then we had this whole sort of middle

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: area of cases, bucket of cases, that were getting bottled up with discovery disputes, and motions were being filed, and it took, you know, many, many, many months.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It was causing all sorts of delay.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So that was the reason that we began the scheduling order initiative, was to deal with that big bucket of cases, which we were trying to prevent.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: From those cases becoming the really old cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so we hired additional law clerks in each of the counties to meet off calendar with the lawyers on resolving these discovery disputes.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And there's a discovery dispute in almost every case.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So the lawyers have an independent obligation to meet together.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And try to resolve the dispute among themselves.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And sometimes that happens, but sometimes we need to.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So we've devoted a lot of resources.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: to get those issues resolved.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that, Judge.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks for specific steps DAs and defense can take. Zayas recommends active participation in discovery conferences, having high-level DA executives available with authority to resolve cases, and notes DAs are doing this.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks what DAs and defense bar can do now to speed dispositions

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In terms of the system actors necessary to make your new protocols effective,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: are there specific steps that the district attorneys and the defense bar can take right now to improve the speed at which cases reach a disposition?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, being active participants in those discovery conferences, with respect to the old cases, we have an old case conferencing so that any of the old cases,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: having the district attorney's offices, having a high-level executive available for those conferences.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: A high-level executive who has the authority to resolve cases, if an offer is made, instead of having to adjourn it to see whether the higher-level district attorneys will approve it.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So those are some of the things that will be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And the district attorney's offices are doing that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers references Zayas's NY Law Journal piece on early suppression hearings and asks what steps have been taken and whether hearings are occurring earlier. Zayas describes the Queens model, doubling of hearings in most counties, and links to increased dispositions.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions early pretrial suppression hearings and data

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In the piece you published in the New York Law Journal, you emphasize the importance of conducting pretrial suppression hearings early in the proceedings because they bring information to light that spurs settlement talks.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What specific steps have you taken to ensure that pretrial hearings happen earlier in the life of a case?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And do you have data on whether these hearings are actually occurring earlier?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So the example that showed that this was a very helpful way of resolving cases early was what they did in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So Queens was the only county when I was the administrative judge there, which didn't wait until the trial date to do suppression hearings.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so we use that example because what happens in those hearings is the judge gets an opportunity to hear the police testify, to get a real sense of what the case is about.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so it's a really important and fertile opportunity for the judge to see the case, for the district attorneys to see the case, for the defense attorney to see the case.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And oftentimes after a hearing, a case might get resolved.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So we always thought, why wait till the end of right before trial?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And let's.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: start this litigation right then.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so as part of the initiative, we've admonished, directed, cajoled,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: pushed the administrative judges to get their judges to hold hearings well before trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And the stats that we received, I have them here.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I don't want to waste two minutes looking for them.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But I will say that I think probably the number of hearings in most of the counties has doubled from the time that we issued that the initiative started.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And we think that's important because those also, I think, are probably also responsible for the increase in the dispositions that we're receiving.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And when you receive a disposition, that's, you know, one less defendant on Rikers Island, one less accused on Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Judge.

chapters.summary: Morano asks whether focusing on a predetermined jail population of 4,000 risks letting capacity dictate criminal justice policy. Zayas says it's aspirational and both goals can coexist without short-circuiting justice.
chapters.title: Councilmember Morano questions Judge Zayas on 4,000 jail population goal vs. justice

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to yield myself to allow my colleagues to ask some questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to first hear from Councilmember Murano, followed by Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you for convening this hearing on such an important topic.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And, Judge, as the council member that represents the only borough that routinely elects both Democrat and Republican judges, I can attest to how widely respected you are by jurists on both sides of the aisle.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And hearing your testimony, it's easy to understand why.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I had a couple quick questions based on your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You stated that

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One of the goals is reducing the jail population to around 4,000 so that it can be accommodated by the borough-based jails.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And when we questioned the correction commissioner, he indicated the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Shouldn't the criminal justice system be focused on administering justice?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: In a way that's fair and in a way that protects public safety rather than achieving a predetermined jail population?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Are we at risk of allowing the capacity of our jail system to dictate criminal justice policy instead of the other way around?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I don't think we're at risk for that because it is an aspirational goal.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And having, you know, what's that phrase?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If you don't have a goal, you're never going to meet it.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I don't think that's the, you could probably say it better than me.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The 4,000 number I will concede does not adequately take into account spikes or increases in criminal activity, violent crimes.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But all things being conceded, I think it's a reasonable thing for...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Folks to do is to have a goal of 4,000 and let's see how we're able to get there.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I don't think justice is being short-circuited or jeopardized by it.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I think

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We can have a goal and also make sure that justice is done without,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: you know, if you're suggesting that we would just start releasing people from jail just to reach the 4,000 number, then I completely get your point.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But otherwise, I think we could do both things at the same time.

chapters.summary: Morano raises concerns about civil court judges without criminal law experience being assigned to criminal court, especially arraignment parts. Zayas says criminal law experience is weighted heavily and such assignments only happen when no other option exists.
chapters.title: Councilmember Morano questions judge assignments and criminal law experience

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One thing that I think Staten Islanders who, and Manhattanites, for instance, may share the same objection is that someone who's elected

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: as a civil court judge on Staten Island because of their expertise in criminal court, in civil court issues and things like that, ends up hearing criminal cases in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The Manhattanite who ends up appearing before this judge may not be voting for that person and may not want them deciding cases.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And the Staten Islander that elected a civil court judge may not want that person hearing criminal concerns.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And one concern I hear from practitioners is that not every judge assigned to criminal court has substantial criminal law experience or even sought that assignment.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How does OCA determine who serves in criminal court, particularly in arraignment parts where judges make critical release decisions?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Should criminal law experience receive greater weight in those assignments?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So, yeah, and they do.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But the question is, what do we do when we don't have enough criminal law?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Experienced lower court judges to sit in those parts.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So I think most recently a civil court judge in Staten Island was elected, but that civil court judge had

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: criminal law experience, was a former prosecutor or defense attorney, and naturally that's where we put him, not in civil court, but in criminal.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I will say this.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I was a practitioner for nine years doing exclusively criminal cases, and I then got hired as a law clerk and then did exclusively civil cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Smart lawyers can make that adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so, and I'm not saying this, you know, criminal law is, you know, rocket science, but I think that people who practice law can make that adjustment, you know, that we typically would assign a mentor to those.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And it's only done, so we would only put a civil court judge who has no criminal law experience in a criminal part when there is no

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: other option.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So for example, in typically in these transition years when a new mayor comes into office,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: there is a shortage of judges because that new mayor now has to create a new committee and has to vet the applicants.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so there's always this sort of one-year delay where we're short on judges.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so that might happen mostly in those transition years after a new mayor is elected.

chapters.summary: Morano asks what one reform outside the courts would have the greatest impact. Zayas says empowering judges to move cases to trial over objections from prosecutors or defense attorneys.
chapters.title: Councilmember Morano asks Judge Zayas for single most impactful reform

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You identify discovery compliance, competency restoration, plea negotiations, defense counsel resources, and prosecutorial practices as major causes of delay.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I agree with you on everything.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Many of which, as you pointed out, are outside the judiciary's control.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If you could change just one thing outside the courts tomorrow, what single reform would have the greatest impact on reducing delays?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: That's a good question.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, I, judges need to be empowered to move a case to trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And the case law does not allow a judge to move a case to trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Even though the parties have, may I continue?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yeah, that's for me.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: to give judges more power to move a case to trial over the objection of a prosecutor or of a defense attorney.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And judges are reticent to do that sometimes because of the reasons that happen, the reasons that people give for requesting an adjournment, but especially with respect to prosecutors.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Once a prosecutor answers not ready, unless the prosecutor has been warned numerous, numerous, numerous times, you've got to start on this date.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So giving judges some more power to do that.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I may have other questions in the second round, if possible.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers clarifies that the hearing's purpose also helps victims, and that Rikers is not designed to be a prison, so moving court processes along helps those in custody either get released or move to state facilities.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers clarifies hearing purpose addresses victims and Rikers closure

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I just want to, on the record, just clarify just a statement that was made because

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The purpose of this conversation also helps victims as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I think we can all agree that Rikers Island is not designed to be a prison.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so by moving along the court process, it allows for those who are in custody to either if they're to be released or if they're to.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Go on to a state facility.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so I just wanted to clarify that these shouldn't be competing issues, and I'll make sure my colleague understands the purpose of this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And with that, we'll hear from Councilmember Brewer, followed by Councilmember Cabon.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises concerns about detainees not getting to court from Rikers, citing past investigations. Zayas says OCA tracks defendant production, has liaisons with DOC in every county, and can share production statistics.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions Judge Zayas on Rikers-to-court transportation issues

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You obviously are the star of this discussion.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So thank you very much, Judge.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My goodness.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We've been talking about

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've been at this for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I was previously chair of oversight and investigations, and we actually had staff that got on the buses at Rikers and went to the courts.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And this was pre my friend Stanley Richards, but it was always 99% of folks get there.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't get there 99% of the time from Rikers to the court.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I guess my question is that may not be something that the courts track, but I don't know if you do because you have to get up at 5 o'clock in the morning, don't necessarily get breakfast, you don't necessarily get food when you get there that you want to eat, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So there's lots of reasons why one doesn't.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Before Stanley Richards, I don't know what now is, individuals who were supposed to get on, detainees were blamed for not getting on.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's why the court numbers weren't great, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But the buses seem to be a problem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know if that's something that you track, if you have any anecdotal, but it seems to me that that's a slowing up of the process also.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I didn't know what you have to say about that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: First of all, very happy to, I've known Stanley Richards for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Very happy with his.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: About 40 years, like me?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Oh, good.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so I'm not, I didn't hear the statistics you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know, they're not clear.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In other words, in the past, DOC would say, oh, they all get on.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know, 99% of folks get to court.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I see.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But it doesn't always work out like that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering what you have experienced.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that number is probably.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Probably inflated.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, a lot of defendants are alleged to have refused to go to court, so that's one of the reasons.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We don't know.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But we do keep track.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We definitely keep track of the number of defendants who are being delivered to court every day.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We also have...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Increased a collaboration.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So in every county, we have a liaison with the folks at DOCS.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And there are discussions all day long about what's happening with this defendant.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Why is that defendant not coming?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And there's a lot of communication going on.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If a defendant is, for example, on trial or is supposed to be doing something significant in terms of litigation, doing a hearing, et cetera, the liaison is all over that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And there are now ways of determining not only whether they were produced, but the time they were produced, the time the bus pulled into the sally port, the time the accused

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: has been checked in, the area of the holding area that the defendants are in.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And I do think we have stats that we can share with the committee.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks if the transportation and liaison system needs improvement. Zayas says no system is perfect and they are always striving to improve.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer asks whether court production and liaison system needs improvement

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And do you think that this is hopefully, you know, I'm like you, everything will be improved.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Henley Richards.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But do you think that this needs improvement?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I guess that's my question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you think, or do you think the liaisons are doing enough to be able to have the kind of structure you want for the buses and the transportation?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right.
