utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, I, this,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: No system is perfect, so I think we are always going to be striving to improve all of the systems that we put in place, including the court system.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There are things we can improve as well.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about technology issues and e-filing progress. Zayas says e-filing took too long but is rolling out in Supreme Courts, with three counties done or in process, and lower criminal court planned next.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions court technology and electronic filing status

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: On that front, when I would go mostly to family court, not criminal, but always the technology was an issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You mentioned it in your statement about the increasing, hopefully, electronic filing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But generally, is the technology something that is up to par?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you find that that's an issue, or is that something that you find is satisfactory?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, it took us too long to get e-filing into the criminal side of things.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's now completely done?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's not completely done, but it's getting there.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So Manhattan is next or something.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, look, the original e-filing statute.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Required us to get permission from the legislature every time we wanted to do it in another court.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So we got a bill passed that essentially allowed the chief administrative judge to decide wholesale what courts should be done next.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so we're doing that in Supreme Court.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We're going to do it in lower criminal court as well.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And I think three

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: of the counties now in Supreme Court in the city, either in the process or have been done already.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about video proceedings between Rikers and courts, noting past technical failures. Zayas acknowledges issues with DOC computer systems causing virtual proceeding disruptions but says improvements are being made.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer asks about video technology between Rikers and courts

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are there other technology issues that are helpful, or that's the main one?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: No, there are all sorts of technological issues that we're considering that will speed along the calendar call.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So there are things that we're exploring.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There are things that we're doing with even with docs that will tell us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what I'm wondering.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, with docs that will tell us, you know, if a defendant says, I'm not coming to court, they immediately begin a, and especially if it's their appearance is really important.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Important because they're in the middle of a trial and a jury is waiting for them.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There's a videotape that's taken and sent immediately to the judge and that person, you know, whether or not that person voluntarily or refused to come to court gets disclosed to the judge.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Then I'll stop.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are there other video possibilities between Rikers and the courts that are used or is that something that, because I have to say, not you, but in family court, I was there and every time just about something would happen with the video, we'd have to stop, fix it, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I don't know if that's the situation with you, but it was constant.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, typically, Docs has been working on...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It was always on, this is going to sound like,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm blaming them for everything.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But our computer systems are all up to par.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: There were some...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Work that needed to be done with the computer systems at Rikers because we would often be in the middle of a, these are virtual proceedings, right?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yes, I know.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And sometimes they would just shut off or they would bring the wrong defendant to the wrong computer and things like that would happen.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That all slows down the system.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what I'm asking.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Cabán raises the issue of judges increasing bail at Supreme Court arraignment for defendants who appeared as required, asking what OCA is doing to stop judges from abusing bail power. Zayas says he cannot direct judges on individual cases but notes indictment may bring more serious charges.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán questions Judge Zayas on bail increases at Supreme Court arraignment

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next, we'll hear from Councilmember Cabana.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Before asking questions, I just want to state that it is shameful, but also really clarifying to see that the DAs aren't here, because the subject of this oversight hearing is improving court operations, and you would think that the prosecutors would have a vested interest

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: in improving court operations, especially to reduce jail populations.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You would think that they have experiences to bring to the table to talk about, to be a partner in doing this work.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So it's unacceptable that they're not here.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And Judge, thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The last sort of preamble thing I'll say is, we're both lawyers.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Lawyers like to be verbose.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to try to be succinct because I've got a lot of questions, but I'm going to ask you to try to be succinct too.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Judge.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, you know, this is something I saw when I was practicing, and it's still continuing today.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We are seeing that at Supreme Court arraignment, a client who was either released or made bail shows up to their Supreme Court arraignment.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Court arraignment to get charged post-indictment.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And either at the request of the DA or based on the arraignment charges, the judge there increases or sets additional bail conditions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Now, bail is simply to ensure that somebody shows up to court.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The best proof of that is that they showed up to the Supreme Court arraignment.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What are you guys doing to stop judges from abusing their power in that scenario?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They shouldn't be increasing bail.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They showed up to court.

chapters.summary: Cabán argues the bail statute is clear that bail is to ensure court appearance and the defendant is present. Zayas explains judicial independence and discretion. Cabán concludes OCA is not leading judges to follow the bail statute.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán presses Judge Zayas on OCA guidance on bail statute compliance

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, I think it's a good question.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Let me try to succinctly answer two parts of it.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The first is, you know, I don't tell, the system we have is I do not tell judges what to do in their particular cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So they are given discretion.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Judicial independence is important.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But you give guidance on the law.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And the bail statute is clear.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like that is that, I mean, that's an area that's actually not really gray in that sense.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You don't tell them how to make these hard, you know, decisions, but.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The legal, the codified purpose of bail is to ensure somebody shows up to court.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And they're standing there.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So there are some judges who believe that once, if there are just criminal charges and a felony complaint,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: that, I mean, I don't know the cases you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We don't know the details.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But sometimes when a defendant gets indicted, that defendant is indicted.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: now getting indicted for more serious charges than the person, the original bail that was set.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And maybe things happened in between those two dates.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so there might have been...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Listen, I'm saying they didn't pick up any new cases.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They're not showing up with...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's an indictment based on the original facts that they were arraigned on in criminal court.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think what I'm taking away from here is that OCA is not holding their responsibility to not just advise, but lead the judges to follow the bail statute.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to move on to another question, but I do think that that's pretty unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They're showing up to court.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about families unable to pay partially secured bonds on nights and weekends, forcing people to wait. Zayas confirms a plan is in place to train clerks to handle these on weekends and holidays in each county.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán questions partially secured bond processing on nights and weekends

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Another common problem that we're seeing is that

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: families are unable to pay partially secured bonds on nights and weekends, which means people who could be released after bail is set or really soon after that have to wait for a clerk to swear out the PSB during the day or during the week.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Will you commit to remedying that?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Will you commit to making sure that we can do these things on nights and weekends?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Because we should be.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so what's the what's the plan for that?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We have a plan in place.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We met with some of the advocates recently who brought that to our attention, and we are going to make that happen.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What do you know?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you share what the plan is?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The plan is to make sure that the clerks on weekends and holidays are able to...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: are required to take those.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, especially for like a three-day weekend, why should someone stay in jail for three more days?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I agree.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: This was recently brought to our attention, and the plan is to make it happen.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: To train the clerks to make it to happen, and it will happen in each of the counties.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about judge training on out-of-state fugitive warrants from neighboring states, noting they take months to resolve. Zayas says training covers exercising discretion including release options, and offers to check for data.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán questions out-of-state fugitive warrant training and release

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm trying to be selective here with some of my questions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I know I don't have a ton of time.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Oh, I want to ask you about...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: about out-of-state, about fugitive warrants.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I want to know what the current training is for judges on out-of-state fugitive warrants, because OCA can train judges to, for example, to release clients on fugitive warrants from at least the neighboring states, like Pennsylvania and New Jersey, for example, because

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They take oftentimes like around a month, sometimes more to resolve, even when there's no local hold.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's totally unnecessary.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And, you know, those clients, when they're not, when the bail isn't set, when they're not remanded, they're almost always going back to those neighboring states, New Jersey and Pennsylvania, to clear those warrants.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So can you talk about the current training and advising on how you handle, particularly fugitive warrants from like New Jersey and Pennsylvania?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Considering the data that shows that these folks, when they are released, they're going to clear those warrants.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, the training is that there didn't used to be the right to a release in those days.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so the training is that they should be exercising their discretion based on all of the facts.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: in circumstances in the case to consider their options, one of which is release.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Do you have data on how often that's happening?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then I'll hand it back to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm not sure we do have data, but we'll get back to you on it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Salaam cites DOC projections of 8,800 by June 2027 and asks what reduction Zayas projects. Zayas questions the projection, notes current population is 6,000 and trending down, and says clearance rates are improving with dispositions exceeding filings.
chapters.title: Councilmember Salaam questions Judge Zayas on jail population projections and case management impact

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We have questions from Councilmember Salam.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yes, thank you, Chair, and thank you for your testimony so far, Judge.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Just a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: DOC projects that the jail population could reach 8,800 by June 2027, while borough-based jails can hold roughly 4,160.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: persons.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Given your case management reforms, what specific reduction in length of stay or population do you project by the August 2027 deadline?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And what is that projection based on?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, did you say 8,000?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Oh, no, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I said it.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I don't know where you're getting that number from.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Right now it's 6,000.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We haven't projected that it's going to be 8,000.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So it's going down.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: This is if it doesn't close.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: So, you know, we're kind of like pushing it out, looking and seeing the data and saying, okay, well, based on DOC's report, they're basically saying that

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: by June of 2027, we would reach a population of 8,800 persons.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I'm a little surprised at that because now it's 6,000.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: You know, I mean, maybe if there's...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's an incredible increase in violence.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: It's from a report in 2025 that they were projecting.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, I doubt the premise of that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But the more that the...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The more that the courts collaborate with the stakeholders, the DAs, as well as the defense bar, and if the trends are continuing where there are

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: consistently more dispositions than filings.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So filings have been down.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If that trend continues,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: You know, whether we're going to reach 4,000, I can't guarantee that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But the– and you should also know that it's not just the cases that should get counted.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's the number of the accused that's actually in jail because some people who are on rankers have multiple cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So, for example, right now I think the defendants with cases over two years old, I think the number is around 502.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But the number of actual defendants is at 460-something.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so that's the number that we think should really be looked at because those are the number of people in Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I wish I could give you more information.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: about what our prediction is with respect to that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: All I can say is that we're working hard to reduce the number of days that it takes to resolve a case.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And the trends in every area are that we're doing that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The clearance, what's called the clearance rate, is getting better, which means that the difference between filings and dispositions.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Every time we have a case where dispositions are way greater than filings, that means that we've just reduced the caseload.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And that's been happening, that happened this year, that's happening in 2026, and it happened in 2025, and it happened in a bigger way than we expected.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Salaam cites data that reducing court appearance intervals by seven days could free 441 beds and asks about current intervals and targets. Zayas explains adjournment timing depends on case stage and reasons, and shorter dates matter most when cases are trial-ready.
chapters.title: Councilmember Salaam questions court appearance intervals and bed capacity impact

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Mark J.'s data shows reducing the interval between court appearances for long stayers by seven days could free roughly 441 beds.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What is the current average interval?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What's your target?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What is your target?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I don't have my glasses on.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And what concretely is preventing courts from achieving it today?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, I could give you mine if you'd like.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So on that, you know, every time a case is on, there might be a different day, a different reason for an adjournment.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So I have never bought in, and I've been doing this for a long time, with the idea that if you reduce a case, the adjournment time, that that will have any effect on anything.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It really depends on what stage is the case in, right?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Are we trying to finish discovery?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Are we trying to get a decision on a motion?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Or are we at a point now where everything's been done and we're trying to get a case tried?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And in those cases, I prefer and we encourage our judges to set shorter adjournment dates.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But sometimes a lawyer will come in and say, because we've got all these programs going on and we've already committed this very busy lawyer to try a case in Brooklyn, and we know that case is going to be a four- or five-week case.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: For us, it doesn't make sense adjourning that person's case in the other county to a 30-day period when we know that lawyer is going to be on trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: If we find out that lawyer is not on trial, we then can advance the case, get that lawyer over, and get that case tried.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So it's really like there are moments that really matter.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And one is, has discovery been complete?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Have the hearings been done?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Is this case really ready for trial?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: That's when the 30-day adjournments really matter.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so we've been encouraging judges to do that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But there's so many reasons.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Like, let's say a defendant, they're waiting for DNA results, and the prosecutor is saying, we're not getting those DNA results for 45 days or 60 days, which is too long.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And one of the things that I think would be helpful is a bigger investment in the forensic units of New York City to more speedily resolve these DNA things.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But if a doctor is telling the judge, you're not getting these DNA results for 60 days, why adjourn a case for 30 days when everybody knows that DNA evidence

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: is going to be very important and is going to have an impact on how the case gets resolved?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'm not sure if I may just one last one.

chapters.summary: Salaam asks about measurable changes from the October 2024 citywide calendaring system and off-calendaring conferences. Zayas says disposition times have been reduced and offers to provide specific numbers.
chapters.title: Councilmember Salaam questions citywide calendaring system and measurable changes

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The citywide calendaring system and off-calendaring conference were announced in October of 2024.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What measurable change in adjournment rates or time to disposition have you seen since implementation, and how many courtrooms or parts are actually using those protocols?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yeah, so the time has been reduced from the time of dispositions.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We could get you those numbers.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about establishing firm trial dates to prevent cases from lingering. Zayas explains trial dates are set once discovery and hearings are complete, with cross-county coordination prioritizing oldest incarcerated cases.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions Judge Zayas on firm trial dates and case management backstops

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Judge, are you taking any steps to establish firm trial dates or some other backstop to ensure cases don't linger?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Linger on indefinitely?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So once, you know, we don't establish a trial date until discovery is complete.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: That's why we've been focusing a lot upon the completion of discovery and then the hearings.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But judges are

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: scheduling trial dates as soon as you can say the case is now ready for trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Not ready in the sense that it's going to go to trial right away, but all the things that needed to get done before trial has gotten done.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so that's happening, and our judges are doing that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: You know, the other thing that gets considered is coordinating between busy prosecutors and busy defense attorneys.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Even though all of those things have happened, you know, the DNA is done, the discovery is all complete, hearings are done, lawyers'schedules also can...

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Adversely affect the ability of a judge to set a date because the lawyer has an older case.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So a lawyer in Queens, who the judge wants that person to go to trial, might have an older case in Brooklyn with a defendant in.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so we have this coordination happening.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We have an initiative which basically makes sure that the oldest

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: case where defendant is incarcerated gets the priority.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So we use our authority to say, wait a minute, Queens judge, do not force that defendant, that lawyer to go to trial because we want this

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: older case, this way older case to go to trial.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So there's even that sort of cross-county collaboration that's happening.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: No, that's good to know.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks if Zayas would advocate for state law changes giving judges more latitude. Zayas says the issue is appellate court precedent, not statute, that prevents judges from moving cases to trial over prosecutor objections.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks about state law changes for judicial calendar management

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Would you advocate for any changes to state law that would give judges more latitude to manage their calendar effectively and hold parties to trial dates?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, it would not really be, I mean, I would advocate the appellate courts to give judges more discretion because there's precedent which precludes a trial court

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: from, if a prosecutor answers not ready.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: To move the case to trial over the objection of a prosecutor.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So it's more decisional law by our appellate courts that have sort of boxed in the judge's ability.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And I don't think that's something that there could be a statutory change to.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about a speedy trial clock for homicides. Zayas hasn't considered it but emphasizes the need for more trained homicide prosecutors, noting the court is always ready when both parties answer ready.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions speedy trial clock for homicide cases

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It's clear from the data that homicide cases are often particularly hard to resolve swiftly.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Would you support a change to state law that would put a speedy trial clock on homicide cases?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: You know, I haven't thought about that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But when it comes to the homicide cases, what I would love to see is the district attorney's offices have more lawyers trained to do those cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Because that is also one of the, you know, if a district attorney's office has five specialized homicide prosecutors, but there are six or seven or eight homicide cases,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: we don't like to see them being able to try the case when everybody else might be ready.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: One of the things I say.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I've been saying since day one when I was appointed chief administrative judge is that the court will always be ready.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: The court will always be ready.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So if the parties come to us and both sides answer ready, you better believe that that case is going to get tried.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: It's only when one side or the other insists that they cannot be ready for one reason or another.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And oftentimes it's related to important matters.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: When it comes from the defense, it's some investigation that has to happen or some other issue.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And judges really loathe to force lawyers to move the case to trial when there are good reasons to not do that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that, Judge.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about reserving community program capacity for court referrals. Zayas strongly agrees, saying it would reduce people waiting on Rikers for beds and that increased city funding would move the needle.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions community programming capacity for court referrals

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And my last question for you is you've indicated that one of the issues standing in the way of reducing the jail population is the lack of suitable community programming

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: available to judges when they're inclined to agree to an alternative to incarceration and detention.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I just want to say on the record, I really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'll private conversation about that, just knowing that there is someone in your role that looks at ATIs as a part of solutions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And resolving some of these matters.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Do you believe it would be advantageous if certain community programs reserved capacity to help accommodate court referrals?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: I mean, and it would reduce the number of people on Rikers Island waiting for those beds.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So once those beds get open, once they're able to send someone to a program,

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: you know, they leave Rikers and that leaves another bed for someone else.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And to the extent the city could increase funding for those programs, that will definitely have a, that would move the needle on the Rikers population.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yeah, and a fan club in the audience.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about delays in treatment court assessments. Zayas says the court has staff ready but lacks enough clinicians for psychosocial evaluations, and increasing those providers would help.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions treatment court assessment delays

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We're still hearing that there are delays associated with cases being referred to treatment courts.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In particular, the delays are related to getting the necessary assessment completed to determine if people are suitable.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: On a city level, what can be done to make treatment courts run more efficiently and effectively?

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: So there are clinicians that sometimes do these reports, especially in mental health court, which makes recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And sometimes there's just not enough clinicians to do that.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We, on our end, have been hiring resource coordinators, all of the necessary parties.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We're ready.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: We have all of our staff in place ready to take on more cases.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: But the folks who do, and it's not the court system, the folks who do the psychosocial and the recommendations for

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: eligibility for treatment are not provided by either district attorney's offices or other nonprofits.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: And so increasing the number of those groups would be helpful as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: George, I thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers thanks Judge Zayas for his candid and thoughtful testimony, concluding the first panel.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers concludes first panel and thanks Judge Zayas

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We're done with this panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I really appreciate all of your candid responses today, and I think it was thoughtful and helpful for the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Thanks for having me.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers calls up Deanna Logan (MOCJ Director), Nora Daniel (MOCJ Chief of Staff), Andrew Batello (NYPD), noting DAs were expected but could not attend, and looks forward to their written testimony.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers calls up second panel: MOCJ, NYPD, and notes DA absence

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We're going to call up our second panel, which includes Deanna Logan, Director for the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice, Nora Daniel, Chief of Staff for the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Andrew Batello, Executive Director of Special Projects and Operations with NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We also had expected to have the district attorneys on this panel and had been in coordination with several offices, and last minute they were not able to make it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So look forward to reading their testimony and hope to have them in an upcoming hearing on this topic as we continue to move this conversation forward.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So if the names that I've...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Called out can come to the dais to be sworn in by council.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'd appreciate it.

chapters.summary: Council staff swears in the second panel, including Deanna Logan, Nora Daniel, and Andrew Batello, who affirm to tell the truth.
chapters.title: Second panel sworn in by council staff

utterances.speaker: Judge Joseph A. Zayas
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Like to swear in this witness panel?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you could all please raise your right hands.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks why MOCJ failed to access over $50 million allocated to the NYC Assigned Counsel Plan since 2018. Logan explains the historical infrastructure challenges and ongoing efforts to build capacity and access funds.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions MOCJ on $50 million assigned counsel plan funding gap

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm not sure if you all were able to listen in to Judge Zaya's testimony and questioning, but I wanted to give a few moments to see if so, if you had any reactions to any of the things that were discussed in that panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: I thought it was a great, robust panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: And we're looking forward to the questions that you have for us so that we can help illuminate more of what's happening with case processing.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Well, I'm going to start with a question that came to mind as he was reading his testimony.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: One was that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I'll just read the line from the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But as a recent report from the New York City Bar Association highlighted, the New York City Assigned Counsel Plan, ACP, administration is understaffed and under-resourced

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: and is thus unable to provide panel attorneys with the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In fact, according to the Bar Association's report, since 2018, the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice has failed to access over $50 million that

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Indigent Legal Services has allocated to the NYCACP under two separate contracts.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I just would like to get an understanding as to why Mach J has failed to pull down that $50 million.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So I think we have to step a little bit back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: The assigned counsel plan is the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Supervisory portion that sits in Machte that has two administrators that are appointed by the courts and then has supervising

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: attorneys that support the individual counsel that are appointed to the 18B panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So the actual attorneys that provide indigent legal services to clients are not in the assigned counsel plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: They are the ones that are appointed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Back in 2018, the assigned council plan was part of the mayor's office of criminal justice, which was also within City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: As the ability to pull down funds for City Hall was hampered by not having a dedicated infrastructure, that...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: change, that infrastructure that needed to happen, started in 2022 when Mach J

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: decided that if it was going to meet the needs that council and our constituents identified, which was not only making sure that the assigned council plan had resources, but also that we were able to pay our

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: partner providers on time that we needed to build infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Since 2022, as council members, we have been in this work of building the infrastructure and moving the mayor's office of criminal justice into its own agency

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: to be able to access the funds that are necessary to do the things that need to happen for not only the assigned council plan, but also for the partners that provide some of the most critical essential services in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: In terms of now...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: accessing those funds as an agency, the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice, in its budget code, can access the funds that now come from the state, because that's where much of the funding comes, into the Mayor's Office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: However, those funds have to then be put into Mach J's budget exactly, and so the budget is then...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: accessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Much of the funds may not be drawn down into our budget.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So we spend what is actually in our budget to support the assigned council plan

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: and work with the indigent legal services as well as bar associations to really think about what the evolution of the assigned council plan should be and how it should be operating and working.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks how much of the $50 million is available to spend. Logan explains the budget allocation process and says they are drawing down available funds, offering to provide full budget details.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers presses MOCJ on how much of $50 million is available now

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So how much of the $50 million is there to be spent now?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: The assigned council budget is approximately...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Give me one second.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm looking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: My CFO was here, and I just wanted to make sure because those numbers are changed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: But I will get you the full budget for them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: But we are pulling down from the funds that are available.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And why is it not the complete 50?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: The allocated budget has to match what is the actual approved and assigned budget.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So each budget has the budget line of the personnel and then the OTPs budget that can be spent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So we will draw down from that budget and spend it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So things like training are now added in so that the assigned council plan members can go to the trainings with the 18B plan attorney so that they can understand the needs and also that they can deliver training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So we started making sure that the assigned council plan could deliver their own trainings as well as working with indigent defense providers in the portfolio where they are able to have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Plan members attend trainings that are being offered by other provider organizations as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks NYPD to describe the process for officers uploading evidence for transfer to prosecutors. The NYPD representative details personnel structures, escalation paths, and three primary transfer mechanisms: DA portal, ECMS, and evidence.com.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions NYPD on evidence upload and transfer process to prosecutors

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next question is for PD.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you please describe the process for officers?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: To upload evidence so it can be transferred to prosecutors?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair, Council Members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: NYPD is grateful for the opportunity to be here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I guess I will start on the personnel front, and then I'll segue into the technological or the production front.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So NYPD policy foundationally is that the arresting officer is primarily responsible for the transmission of material at the arrest arraignment processing window.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: They are the one conferring with the early case assessment bureau that's on the district attorney side for the screening of that case, strength of the prosecution, weakness of the prosecution, and whatever additional materials.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: May be needed by the prosecution at that time, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: It is an open dialogue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Outside of that, if there are issues with discovery subsequent to that arraignment window,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: NYPD has dedicated personnel in every command citywide, numerous personnel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There's a primary contact and a secondary contact that each of our local prosecuting agencies have a full and exhaustive list of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And they are to be, you know, contacted if there are any issues with discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: or contacting the arresting officer for any number of reasons, vacation or they're just not available or not answering the phone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And then on top of that, to a higher level of degree or escalation, there's a centralized discovery liaison unit that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: turns out of the office of the chief of department under the chief of department.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: They are embedded in each of the five local prosecuting agencies, and they also work hand-in-hand with the district attorneys on two types of cases, and I can get into that if you'd like me to, or two buckets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: primarily.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I guess we'll stay on the same track of that escalation, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So there's an arraignment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There's that initial conferral between NYPD and their respective, you know, screening ADA and ECAB in the district attorney's office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Subsequent to arraignment, they don't have the ability to reach or they're not having success for whatever reason with the dedicated command level personnel, they would reach out to the discovery liaison unit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So that would be a DA request.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So that's the first bucket.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That could be a particular request on any case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That could be a request because there's a 180-80 concern.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Trying to meet grand jury requirements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That could be a speedy trial concern or any number or any reason why any ADA citywide needs assistance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The second bucket.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: of cases that the discovery liaison unit assists with, and this is a larger bucket, is automatic or proactive cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I mentioned that there is a discovery liaison unit embedded in each of our five local district attorney offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Those members of service are constantly monitoring the live arrest feed,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: and there are certain arrest types or crime classifications that the Discovery Liaison Unit will assist with automatically.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: They are...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, the more complex type of arrests that have more voluminous discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Primarily speaking, the DAs, the DLUs assist the district attorneys proactively with homicides, with attempted murders, attempted homicides, shootings, non-fatal shootings, or confirmed shots fired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Criminal possession of a weapon in the second degree, you know, that coincides with the 2022 initiative that OCA launched with the Supreme Court specialized gun parts, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So the DLUs are there to facilitate discovery on those cases in the hopes of expediting, you know, the case processing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: On that as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So that pretty much sums up and also robbery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: In the first degree.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So that pretty much sums up the second bucket of DLU.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Now, I know that was seem like a lot, but there's also now like a technological aspect of this, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: How are the materials being transmitted to the district attorney's offices?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So there are some outliers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: But there are three primary transfer mediums or points of workflow where NYPD has established bridges

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: with the local prosecuting agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So the first one, and this is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Most foundational to ECAB, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Because it has the underlying initial arrest documents that is absolutely needed for comprehensive screening by the district attorneys, the DA portal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So that transfer mechanism receives

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: the complaint report from NYPD, the most basic report that contains, you know, the pedigree identifying information of the incident, the time, the location, the occurrence, the crime, the allegations, so on and so forth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The accompanying arrest report that corresponds with that complaint, who, which individual has been arrested pursuant to that complaint.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Property clerk invoices, when there's an arrest, there's sometimes an extensive amount, sometimes not so much amount of property or evidence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: that is inventoried by the police department, activity logs, and there are also any number of types of materials that can be transferred.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Scratch paperwork, anything else that may exist in that arrest processing arraignment window, the policy,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The guidance, the technology dictates that it's automatically shared with the district attorney's office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The second mechanism is called the enterprise case management system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That is, if you think about arrests that have an investigative nature to it, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So sometimes there's just a patrol arrest and it's very simple, but a lot of times, right, the NYPD has...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Detective Bureau, investigators, intelligence bureau, field intelligence officers, and other investigatory support units.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That is where their casework is documented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Summary of the work that they do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Attachments to those notes or DD5s as NYPD and the district attorneys refer to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So attachments may be photographs, they may be videos, they may be additional documents in PDF form or in JPEG form for photos.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That is automatically shared with the district attorney's office upon the investigator sharing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Now, I just want to touch on

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The first two mediums that I just spoke about, the DA API portal and the ECMS portal,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: over the years, we've worked with the DA's collaborator, not only to build the medium that both sides are speaking to each other for integration into the case management system, but also for automation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So since criminal justice reform to where we are currently, now as the materials hit the system, they are automatically pushed or sent to the respective DA office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The DA is notified of that and the ADA and the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Paralegal and the discovery and the support staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: gets the notification, they're able to review it, and then obviously turn that over subsequently down to defense counsel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The last primary mechanism or method of transfer is evidence.com.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Evidence.com houses

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: body-worn camera of all of our members of service and fleet camera as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Some of our RMPs have docked dash cams, as they're often referred to, and that, again, is sent to the district attorney's offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: automatically.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And that's, again, that occurs pre-arrangement in the screening process, and then as the case continues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So that's the general overview.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks if NYPD tracks whether officers upload evidence timely. The NYPD representative explains the arraignment window dialogue, post-arraignment obstacles, and that DAs utilize designated personnel and discovery liaison units to meet statutory deadlines.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks if NYPD tracks timely evidence uploads

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: No, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: That was very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Does the department track whether officers are timely in uploading their evidence?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So as I mentioned, in that arrest arraignment window, and there's that dialogue, there are thresholds of what the DA set

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: as necessary to properly screen a case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That may be contingent on a crime classification.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: It may be contingent on what actually occurred in the complaint incident or the arrest incident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: But there's a dialogue there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: As we look post-arrangement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are obstacles in the ability to do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: After that arraignment, if it's arraigned, we assume it is not declined or deferred.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So we see into that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We know when a case is not arraigned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We know when it's declined.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: When we speak about speedy trial issues,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, there are a number of reasons or scenarios that could alter the clock.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Could the case be subsequently dismissed?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Does or are there waivers of time?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Where are we exactly in that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I don't think it's.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: It's linear, you know, 90 days for a misdemeanor, 180 days for a felony on where that is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And because of that, you know,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The district attorneys have been great partners in utilizing the personnel that have been designated and are responsible for discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: What I mean is when...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Discovery materials are outstanding or they want to ascertain or verify if there are additional materials

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: outside of the arresting officer subsequent to arraignment, they do utilize the command specific personnel and then also the discovery liaison unit to meet those deadlines

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: that are defined statutorily.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that clarification.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks what happens when officers don't upload evidence timely and whether supervisors are informed. The NYPD representative says they investigate issues brought to light by DAs and address them by severity, acknowledging some delays occur due to voluminous or inaccessible materials.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions consequences for officers who don't upload evidence timely

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And just in the event that an offer does not upload the evidence in a timely way, what happens?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Are supervisors informed?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Are there any other actions taken at all?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We speak with the district attorneys at a higher level than the arresting officer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And if something is brought to light, we look into the case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Are there times where there is an undue delay, unfortunately?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are times where that occurs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: However, are there scenarios where it is extremely voluminous on the police department side to gather the materials that causes speedy trial to go?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Are there times where it's taking the district attorney's office time to review a voluminous case file and they're exercising due diligence in doing so?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I think, though, you know, there are times where the material may not exist yet, a complex report.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, the chief judge alluded to that in his testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are times where the materials aren't readily accessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, the mechanisms in place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: transfer the materials as soon as they exist and are accessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And, you know, if the department does find something, then it's dealt with in accordance with the severity of the issue.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks if NYPD can definitively say it transmits evidence to DAs within statutory timeframes. The NYPD representative says yes with the caveat that materials must be accessible, noting some materials like 911 records have backlogs.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks if NYPD definitively meets discovery transmission timeframes

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As I'm sure you're aware, state law considers any material in the possession of law enforcement to also be in the possession of the prosecution.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you definitively say that NYPD is transmitting?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Evidence to the district attorneys within a time frame that enables them to meet the disclosure deadlines imposed by state law, including for incarcerated defendants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Sure, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Thank you, Anna.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If you could just start with a yes and no and then go into it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The department.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You want me to read it again?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: This is an important question that I really want to know the answer to.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As I'm sure you're aware, state law considers any material in the possession of law enforcement to also be in the possession of the prosecution.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you definitively say that...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The NYPD is transmitting evidence to district attorneys within a time frame that enables them to meet the disclosure deadlines imposed by state law, including for incarcerated defendants.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So I ask that you start with a yes and no first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And I will, and I appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Yes, with the caveat that the material needs to be accessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I explained materials such as the complaint report and the arrest report and vouchers and body-worn camera and dash cam and case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Enterprise case management fives and attachments that exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Unfortunately, right, there's two things at play here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There's accessibility, meaning certain materials are more difficult to retrieve, review, and produce than other materials that relate to the arrest.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That is the first thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The second...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: aspect of this is that when you look at the discovery statute and this arraignment window and 20 days for an incarcerated and 35 days for an at liberty

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: discovery and evidence is not static meaning that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are cases where individuals, including those that are at liberty and incarcerated,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: it is dynamic, meaning that there is subsequent discovery that is being produced as the case adjudication, as the criminal prosecution progresses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So the systems that we have in place with the automation that was discussed are designed to send that to the DAs as they become in existence, as they are made final, as they are made whole.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: They go directly to the DAs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So if a report.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Or a laboratory report on the PD side is signed off on and finalized at day 10.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Or even outside of those windows.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That gets uploaded into the case and automatically shared.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Sometimes the materials do not exist or don't have the ability to go to the DAs in that time frame.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are other materials that take longer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I know Mach J is in on this effort with the district attorneys as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There's an open dialogue here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: instance, 911 records, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There is a backlog on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We take it very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We are doing everything we can on that, but I cannot say affirmatively that we're producing every single material related to the case in the timeframe.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about DLU staffing, civilian assignments, and case type limitations. The NYPD representative details one lieutenant, five sergeants, 25 officers/detectives, all uniform, split across five DA offices, handling proactive cases and DA requests.
