chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions NYPD discovery liaison unit staffing and case limits

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How many officers are assigned to discovery liaison units in the district attorney's offices within each borough?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Are there any civilians assigned to those units?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Are there limits in place as to what types of cases those liaisons can work on?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And if so, why are those limitations established and are there differences between the boroughs?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to do my best to hit a few of those, and if I miss anything, please just tell me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So it's a strictly uniform position.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are no civilians assigned to the Discovery Liaison Unit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There is one lieutenant who oversees...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The unit from a central position.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That lieutenant is based in one police plaza.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And then there are five sergeants also assigned to the discovery liaison unit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Five sergeants would be one in each respective county district attorney office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And then there are 25 additional uniforms in the rank of police officer and detective.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That varies a little bit on the headcount for each of those ranks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And those 25 are split amongst the DA offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Now, I think you asked for the case types.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I touched on it a little earlier.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I think it's easiest to think about it in two primary buckets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There's proactive or automatic case assistance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That's contingent upon...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The arrest type, the arrest charge, murders, attempted murders, shootings, shots fired, criminal possession of a weapon in the second degree, robbery first degree.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And then there are the DA requests.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There are times where the prosecution has difficulty obtaining materials that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: materials that or arrests or materials relating to arrests that aren't in those proactive nature of cases, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: This is a DA request.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So the arresting officer is maybe not available or there is a speedy trial issue or a grand jury.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Need to get that material as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So then they also work on a DA request.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So the district attorney's offices have guidance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: They're great partners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: They utilize them whenever needed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And then they're

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: There is direct case assistance embedded in each of the DA's offices when they need assistance.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks if DA requests for additional DLU staff have been met. The NYPD representative says DLU is at its highest staffing ever since 2021, balances operational needs with resources, and will relay the council's concerns to upper executives.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks if NYPD has met DA requests for additional discovery liaison staff

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We understand that district attorneys have requested additional NYPD staff in those units.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Have those requests been met?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: What I can say is DLU at this juncture is the highest staffing level of all time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: DLU has been in existence since 2021.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: NYPD implemented DLU at its own volition preemptively to essentially assist and facilitate with, as the chief judge said, right, onerous discovery laws

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: with the hopes of facilitating compliance and assisting with a more expeditious case processing mechanism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, the police department is grateful for the district attorney's acknowledgement of just how beneficial the DLUs are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: The DLU staffing right now, you know, is contingent, or rather the caseload is contingent on staffing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, the police department, you know, balances the operational needs with the resources that they currently have allocated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I'm more than happy to bring back to the upper level executives, you know, the sentiment that you stated regarding the DAs requesting more DLUs.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Do you support giving district attorneys access to NYPD databases so they can secure the evidence they need to move prosecutions forward?

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks if NYPD supports giving DAs access to NYPD databases. The NYPD representative says that's above his level but notes existing automated mechanisms already give DAs direct access to case files, and highlights automation improvements.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions NYPD on giving DAs direct database access

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So I can't speak to the overall agency position.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I can speak to what the department has implemented in tandem and collaboration with the DAs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I can speak about the enhancements that have been

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: implemented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, the automation of the DA portal, of the ECMS portal, and evidence.com has drastically improved the discovery workflow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I'd just like to give an example.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So the chief judge mentioned in his testimony,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: that in 2025 dispositions were up between 10 and 11%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I forget the exact number he cited.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: PD worked with the district attorney's office on two main projects and with Mach J as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We automated body-worn camera collection in the evidence.com environment, meaning prior to this, officers had to manually search.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: For the videos, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So if there's a large patrol response, let's say there's an incident, a violent incident where it touches precinct boundaries,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: that arresting officer is now responsible to gather dozens, if not hundreds of body-worn camera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So we leveraged the vendor with

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: with evidence.com to automate that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: We also automated the arrest portal, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: So when you think about NYPD and Mach J and the DA's commitment to continuing to enhance this workflow, right, maybe it's telling that dispositions are up in the same timeframe.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So just trying to get clarification on terms of why you wouldn't be able to speak on behalf of the agency's position on there.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Is it that there's not a position that's established or?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: You know, I...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: That's a very...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Critical issue or it's a large decision, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: What I can say up front is, you know, that's a little bit above my level and my pay grade.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: But there are mechanisms in place that are giving the DAs direct access already to the case file.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about NYPD policy on documenting reasons for denying DATs and whether DAT investigation forms are still used. The NYPD representative says this is an operational decision outside his scope but offers to follow up with data.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán questions NYPD on desk appearance ticket denial documentation

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to Councilmember Caban.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: My questions are for the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to start by sort of the role that NYPD has in relation to the production of people that are in the system.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Just like the DAs have a lot of control in this area, so does the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And unfortunately, we're seeing...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What is, can unequivocally be described as a drastic increase in broken windows policing, which again, there is well over two decades of research that says this stuff does not bear

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: any progress on public safety.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But I want to know about the NYPD's current policy on documenting the reason a DAT was denied.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You know, my understanding is that you guys maybe aren't using DAT investigation forms anymore, which those are the documents that include a lot of the exceptions that aren't in the statute.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But based on the information that I've been getting from folks who work with people in the system, and based on my questioning at previous hearings,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: This does not seem to be what's actually happening.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So can you lay out exactly what the policy is around the document?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: of the reason a DAT or an appearance ticket was denied.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Councilmember, I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Unfortunately, I think you're discussing or talking about an operational decision that is essentially occurring in a command by a uniformed member of service.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I think it's a little, it is outside of the scope that I'm able to attest to here today.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I mean, how many people are going before a judge and how many people are being processed rather getting an appearance ticket, I think falls under the scope of an oversight hearing of

chapters.summary: Cabán argues DAT policy is central to court efficiency and jail population, citing data that over 90% of the time NYPD finds reasons to avoid field DATs, often citing open summons warrants. The NYPD representative says he will follow up with data.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán presses NYPD on DAT denial data and broken windows policing

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: the efficiency of the courts and in reducing the jail population.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You know, you can't get into jail without.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: An arrest being precipitous.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I don't know why you're not able to testify on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And I'll be more than happy to follow up with you with respect to the reasoning and that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: And I'll try to get you data on reasons why.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: DATs are being disqualified.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: If we're able to capture that metric, I would have to check that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And just as a reminder from the testimony from the executive budget hearing around this, the court decision at the state level.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Says when a D.A.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: or an appearance ticket must be given in the field.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And well over 90% of the time, the NYPD is finding a reason or just flouting the law and taking people to the precinct to process them there.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And it's causing a lot of problems.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's not just backing up the system, but also we're seeing really devastating things happen to the people while they're in police custody.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And the reason that was given was that there are other open warrants, but what...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Failed to be disclosed is the data around how many times those open warrants were for the same kind of nonsense, where

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: those open warrants were actually SAP warrants, right, where summons warrants is for taking up an extra seat on the subway, for having an open container, things that should not justify

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: somebody being processed and, quite frankly, categorizing them as, like, we had to process them because they had these open warrants, amounts to misleading fear-mongering.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I would like the data on that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you tell me the most common reason why an otherwise eligible DAT is denied?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Again, I'm going to have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about 2,600 extra officers on summer foot patrols and whether they'll be instructed to make arrests for quality-of-life crimes or refer to services. The NYPD representative says this is operational and he cannot speak to it, offering to follow up.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán questions NYPD on summer foot patrol arrest instructions

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's been reported that the NYPD is surging its presence with 2,600 extra officers on late night and early morning foot patrols this summer.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Will officers be instructed to make arrests on those foot patrols for quality of life crimes?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, again, listing some of these things like loitering, disorderly conduct, parks violations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Again, Councilmember, it's very operational in nature.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I can't speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So you also can't speak to whether these officers will be instructed on how to refer people to services rather than make an arrest?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: Again, I can go back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - NYPD Representative
utterances.text: I can speak to the upper-level executives, find out about if there is any such messaging, and get back to you.

chapters.summary: Cabán states that not being able to speak about how, when, and why arrests are made means NYPD didn't show up prepared, emphasizing everyone has a role to play in system efficiency.
chapters.title: Councilmember Cabán criticizes NYPD for lack of preparation on arrest practices

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I mean, again, you don't get into the courthouse without an arrest.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so not having information about who we are subjecting to a criminal court proceeding, I think, is unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, you didn't show up prepared for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Everybody has their role to play, right?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, you're making the arrest.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: There's a lot of discretion there.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The prosecutors then in ECAB have the ability to DP or decline to prosecute.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They have the ability to move cases along quicker.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The judge has the things that they can do.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Defense attorneys need more resources so that they can help put the cases along more speedily.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But I will say to show up here and not be able to speak about how, when, and why arrests are being made and whether you're being judicious with that really big responsibility means that you didn't show up here prepared.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks why DA and defender data management systems aren't implemented six years after discovery reform. Logan explains vendor capacity challenges, with one DA office served but replication across boroughs failing, and interim workarounds.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions MOCJ on data management system delays since discovery reform

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We're going to pivot now to Mach J.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Under Mayor de Blasio, when discovery reform was passed, Mach J took on the role.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: of coordinating acquisition and installation of new data management systems for DAs and defenders.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Why is it the case that over six years later, those officers do not yet have the function and systems implemented?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So the data management systems have been a challenge to the system, to the criminal legal system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: We worked with all of the stakeholders to determine and identify components that would be able to integrate with the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: current system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: As council is aware, we pursued a vendor not only for the district attorneys, but also for the defense bar in order to actually have the infrastructure that would

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: ingest the information from NYPD and then categorize and save the information and then allow for the prosecutors to then

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: transmit that information to defense bar and then defense bar to receive that information in a coherent fashion because one of the things that mock jay heard was that discovery material was coming to them very

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: um,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: aggregated and not cataloged in a way that made it usable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Unfortunately, in the vendor that was chosen, it seems that there was a capacity challenge so that one of the DA's offices was able to get the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: very targeted work that needed to happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: However, the vendor overestimated the ability to essentially copy the work that it was doing with the first DA's office and go across the system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Having five DA's offices that have very distinct styles as well as distinct systems that they had originally created on their own, the vendor

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: took on more than they could chew in terms of being able to actually then do the same very targeted work that they did for one borough and replicate that in a timely fashion for all the other boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: What we now have are offices that have

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: in the interim built their own internal systems such that they are using the funding that we have advocated for to add personnel and do their own forms of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: workarounds to be able to meet their burden of providing discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: We still are talking to all of the stakeholders about ways to move forward and where there may be opportunities for technological advancements that will move us to a more one-city seamless approach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: to getting the information not only from the NYPD to the DAs, but appropriately to the defense bar so that we can more effectively handle cases.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks what role MOCJ plays in ensuring timely discovery from NYPD and other agencies. Logan describes MOCJ as a 'log jam clearer,' citing an example of working with OCME to expedite DNA report reviews through overtime funding.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers asks MOCJ role in ensuring timely NYPD discovery to DAs

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What role, if any, does Mach J have in ensuring that NYPD and other city agencies are providing discovery in a timely way to the DAs?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Mock J serves kind of as the log jam clearer, as it were.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: We will get information about a challenge, and then we will specifically work with the stakeholders to move that logjam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: For example, we know that the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner is critical in getting DNA evidence and reports on various categories of cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: And we heard that there was some slowdown in getting those reports.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So our team will go in and we'll talk to the OCME.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: And we said, what's going on here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: And we realized that they have senior people that have to review the reports before they go out to the various DA's offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Well, why aren't they being reviewed?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Well, because we have but so many personnel and they don't have enough hours in the day to do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So then for us, we're like, okay, how do we solve this problem?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: We go and we talk to our partners at the Office of Labor Relations and OMB.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: And we say, here is a challenge that is glutting the system and we need to alleviate it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: What do you think about being able to access?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: funds that allow for overtime and making sure that the contracts allow for that in that category of employee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: And together with the stakeholders, we work out the way to get that done so that now the supervisors are able to have the time in the day to review those documents and that they're more timely being provided to the DAs and then the cases can move.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: So that is an example of how we start to move and look at what the challenges are in the system.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks what steps MOCJ has taken to speed up the 730 process for people unfit to stand trial. Jill Starashevsky describes working with OCA to eliminate adjournment delays by requiring clerks to share evaluation reports within 24 hours.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions MOCJ on 730 competency restoration process reforms

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What steps, if any, has Mach J taken to speed up and reform the

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: 730 process for people who may not be competent to stand trial?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: 7.30 is definitely a challenge, and we have worked with stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: I'd actually like the first deputy of our office, since she's the one who helped facilitate this one, to have the opportunity to take her flowers for being able to work through one of the challenges.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: She'd come to the days.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We just need to swear her in.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Can you state your name for the record?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Jill Starashevsky.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Deanna Logan
utterances.text: The issue is that when someone is found unfit for a trial,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: As you know, the process is the person needs to be transferred to OMH facility for restoration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: There is currently a backlog in that process in the fact that there is not enough beds for the transfer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: So what we've been trying to do is to assess different ways to shorten that transfer time for those found unfit, as well as trying to develop a robust...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: We've been exploring outpatient restoration models.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: So we've been working with our partners at CHS and with the DAs and the defenders to try to figure out what we could do there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: And perhaps most importantly, what we've done is we've worked with our partners at OCA to take certain steps to make it so that when the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: evaluation, the 730 evaluation report is given to the court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: There used to be a delay that the court received the report and then there was an adjournment

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: where the report was given to the prosecutor and then the defense attorney would be given it in court and that would then require another adjournment because the defense attorney had to have the opportunity to review the evaluation report.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: Working with our partners, we were able to make it so that now the way it works is when the 730 evaluation report is given to the court, the clerk is required within 24 hours to share that report with both the prosecutor and the defense, and that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: really alleviates...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: sometimes one, sometimes two adjournments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: And so that helps to expedite the court process.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about implementation of Local Law 139 of 2025 requiring ATI assessments within six weeks. Starashevsky says partners have operationalized it but data isn't available yet, and will share when ready.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions MOCJ on Local Law 139 implementation for ATI assessments

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Last session, the council passed Local Law 139 of 2025 to expedite access to court-based alternative to

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: incarceration programs by requiring that within six weeks of an attorney's request,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: An assessment would be conducted to determine if a person is suitable for a program.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you give us an update on where things stand with the implementation of this law?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And are attorneys requesting assessments?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Have you been able to meet the statutory timeline to turn those assessments around within six weeks?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: So that portion of the law was passed last year, and I believe the operational date was this spring, and so our partners have operationalized it within the program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: We don't have the data on that yet, but we will have the data on it shortly.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And you can share it with us once it becomes available.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Do you have an idea of a timeline on that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jill Starashevsky
utterances.text: I'm not sure of a timeline, but we can get it back to you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers thanks and relieves the MOCJ/NYPD panel, then reads public testimony rules and calls up the public defender panel: Youngmi Lee, Wesley Caines, Elizabeth Fisher, Stan German, and Tina Luongo.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers relieves second panel and calls up public defender panel

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Well, thank you to the panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: This panel is relieved.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next, we will have the public defender's...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And that will include, before I call them up, I'll just read this script real quick.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I now open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant at Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And now we'll call our first panel, which includes...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Young Mi Lee with Brooklyn Defenders.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Wes Canes with Bronx Defenders.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Elizabeth Fisher with Neighborhood Defender Services of Harlem.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Stan German, New York's County Defender Services.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And Tina Luongo, the Legal Aid Society.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I know you all are given statements, and the clock is set for five minutes for each of you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You can begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Whatever order you like.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: However you like.

chapters.summary: Luongo frames the problem as systemic, noting the absence of DAs and NYPD from meaningful accountability, highlighting public defender attrition due to low pay while NYPD overtime nears a billion dollars, and calling for investment in services and defender salaries.
chapters.title: Tina Luongo (Legal Aid Society) on systemic causes of court delay and resource misallocation

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I'll go first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I'm Tina Longo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I'm the chief attorney at the Legal Aid Society's criminal defense practice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We are the citywide provider of trial, appellate, parole, and post-conviction work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We have trial offices in each of New York City, and we are celebrating our 150 years serving the city

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: and have been the public defender for the city before Gideon v.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Wainwright.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I have served as the public defender and began my career in the New York City Office of the Legal Aid Society.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: For over 25 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I wanted to start, my colleagues are each going to take specific

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: issues to address.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I sort of wanted to step back and set the sort of groundwork.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: First and foremost, thank you for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: It's critically important, particularly right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: But it is also telling, I think, a little bit

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: about who is not at this table and who has not come before this, you as the chair and this council,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: who is not here to answer questions about desk appearance tickets and over-policing,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Because I think what it says is that people, the district attorneys and the police department, see this as a very narrow issue that you're trying to address, when in fact it is not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: It is actually a systemic problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: What we are trying to, and what we are talking about here, and what we are trying to get to, is what is the problems and what are the solutions that are driving court delay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: But you cannot have that conversation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: You cannot have solutions without identifying the problems and with each stakeholder coming forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: To say, here is what we see, here is the role we play, here are the solutions we implement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: But you're not really getting all of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: You have the core.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: come, and we really do appreciate our judiciary, our chief judge, and our statewide administrative judge for taking proactive steps.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We appreciate that Mach J was here to provide some context.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We appreciate that you heard a lot about this discovery technology.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: And I certainly appreciate our colleagues who come as public defenders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: And every single day right now in courts across this city, our staff are dealing with those delays.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: More importantly, the people we represent, those impacted, are suffering those delays.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: It isn't really about court delay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: It's about intentional decisions made about where we are going to, as a city, invest our time and money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So as we are hearing from the judge, and as my colleagues likely will talk about, we need program services, we need mental health services, our clients need housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Our public defenders need higher salaries.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We have offices right now negotiating contracts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Some of them have, our union has said that they will strike.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We have lost felony attorneys at astronomical clips.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: And I want to say, I will give them this, that the district attorneys also lose

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: felony assistants, DAs that are ready to try serious cases at high rates because of the lack of pay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Because of the lack of keeping up with the cost of living in New York City for people who have dedicated themselves to public service.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: At the same time, though, NYPD's overtime budget is nearing a billion dollars and has almost hit a billion dollars four years in a row.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: At the same time, we are looking at

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: at the attrition of our staff, our social workers, our investigators, our litigation assistants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: NYPD in this city is set to bring on a brand new class.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Those are intentional decisions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: About where we are placing our resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So it is hard for us to think through how do we fund all the solutions we need within the court system

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: and within our offices that must have the staff to meet the challenges that are brought by what is real justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So I say that to set the framework that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: the problem doesn't start at the courthouse door and the solution will never be found in tinkering around the edges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: And I thought I'll turn it over to my colleagues.

chapters.summary: German details the 730 mental health crisis with 5.4-month OMH transfer waits, no-parted trials due to lack of courtrooms, Manhattan sending more people to Rikers than Kings County despite fewer cases, and onerous ATI screening by DA Bragg's office.
chapters.title: Stan German (NY County Defender Services) on 730 crisis, trial capacity, and bail practices in Manhattan

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair, Councilmember Burr, all the council members who have taken part in this important hearing this morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: I think, you know, to just piggyback off what Tina just said, if the city is really committed to closing Rikers Island, it has to build meaningful alternative pathways away from Rikers, which ultimately means far more investment in the community-based services

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: that address root causes, root cases, and break the insidious cycle of criminalization, incarceration, and recidivism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: With that being said, I really want to get and focus on real issues and offer some real solutions because I don't think we really heard solutions from

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: prior testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: So, Madam Chair, you spoke about the 730 crisis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Right now, it is taking someone who has been found unfit and committed to OMH custody about 5.4 months to make it to an OMH hospital.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And that's from the time that the commitment order is signed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: If you tack on about another two or three months in order to get the 730 exam, is one party or the other going to controvert that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We're probably looking at closer to seven or eight months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: That's somebody who is mentally unfit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Is sitting on Rikers Island not getting the service.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: They need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: There are two things that can absolutely happen right now to help alleviate this problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Makche alluded to one of them, which is out restoration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Out restoration can be ordered by a court in particular and suitable cases, but it requires DA consent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The reality is that we worked with Makche to identify cases that we thought would be suitable for out restoration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We sent that list to the district attorney's office in Manhattan, and it was dead on arrival.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: There was no discussion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: There was no consideration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The word back was simply they will not consent to out restoration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: For somebody who is found unfit, regardless of the circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The other tool in the toolbox is OMH.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Now, OMH has a capacity problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We all understand that there aren't enough beds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: But OMH also has statutory authority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: To send folks to other hospitals and other facilities, as long as they have the proper psychiatric care that that person can provide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We are in a crisis right now with mental health and Rikers Island serving essentially as a mental health hospital, which it is not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: So it's going to take creative solutions and creative thinking in order to solve those issues, and those are just two of the matters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: With regard to trial capacity, and after Judge Zayas said what he said about, you know, the court is always ready, I ran out, and I told the judge, well, Judge, what we've seen in Manhattan over the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: last several months is that cases are being what we in the court house call no parted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: What that means is the DA is ready to try the case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We're ready to try the case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We show up into the courtroom and there are no parts available.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: That means there's no way to send this case to be tried.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The reality is that in Supreme Court, New York County last year tried 230 cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Kings comes in at 133.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We've always outperformed the other boroughs when it comes to the number of trials we do in New York County.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We simply need more judges and more resources so we can try these cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Because when a case is no parted, it doesn't just get adjourned for a day or for two days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: It could be adjourned for a month.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: It could be adjourned for two months, vacations, witness availability changes, which is adding to the delay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Bail practice in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: So Kings County does 37% more cases than we do on a yearly basis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Yet New York County sends more people to Rikers Island than Kings County does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And the reality is that 64% of the cases that are being sent to Rikers Island in Manhattan are misdemeanors and nonviolent felonies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: If we're truly going to get that jail population down, we need leadership from Mach J and the city and this chamber and the DAs to figure out how do we off-ramp.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The people who are charged with misdemeanors and nonviolence, so that we're not sending them to Rikers Island and adding to an increased jail population.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And the last...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: issue I want to address is the ATI courtrooms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We have seen increased numbers of individuals who are being accepted into ATI in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: But the process, the process that DA Bragg's office uses to screen individuals is simply onerous, and it's something we don't see in any other borough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We can spend months upon months having to supply medical records to not doctors but to prosecutors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: They require proffers, something no other borough does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Every single person, we have to prepare them, we have to go in, we have to have a meeting, you've got to tell your life story.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And if the answer is no,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We could have spent eight, nine, ten months trying to advocate for somebody to get in, only to be told at the end, it's not going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And so, you know, the Treatment Court Expansion Act, I know that's an Albany issue, but the fact is we have to take mental health

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: away from the DAs being the gatekeepers, let the judges and the courts administrate the same way we did with drug diversion about 15 years ago.

chapters.summary: Caines presents data showing low-level arrests up 16.2% from 2024, 55% from 2019, and 193% from 2021, with subway seat arrests up 3,000% and drug possession arrests up 136%, arguing the city must invest in communities rather than policing.
chapters.title: Wesley Caines (Bronx Defenders) on broken windows policing driving jail population

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair and committee members, for the opportunity to testify before you today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: My name is Wesley Canes, and I am the Deputy Executive Director at the Bronx Defenders and a formerly incarcerated member of the Bronx community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: The Bronx Defenders is a public defender nonprofit that is radically transforming how low-income people in the Bronx are represented in the legal system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Each year, our holistic defense teams defend more than 20,000 low-income Bronx residents in criminal, civil, child welfare, and immigration cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: We appear before this council to testify on how the city can effectively reduce the jail population.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: While discussions about court operations are important, we cannot meaningfully address the jail population without first acknowledging what drives people into the system in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: The population at Rikers is driven by arrests, over-policing, and decades of underinvestment in the communities most impacted by our criminal legal system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: If the city is serious about reducing the jail population and closing Rikers, it must invest in communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: and reduce unnecessary contact with the legal system before people ever reach a courtroom or a jail cell.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: First, the city must end the continued and in fact renewed reliance on broken windows policing that we know does not make communities safer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Far too many New Yorkers continue to be arrested and funnel into our criminal legal system for conduct that poses little to no threat to public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Custodial arrests for offenses such as fare evasion and occupying multiple subway seats do not make communities safer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: After falling approximately 16% on average year over year from 2014 until a record low in 2020,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Low-level policing has been growing steadily ever since.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Low-level arrests in 2025 were up approximately 16.2% from 2024, approximately 55% from where they were in 2019.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: And a staggering 193% from where they were in 2021, totaling the most arrests in a year since 2017.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Recent reports show that the number of people arrested for lying down or taking up more than one seat on the subway

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: went up 3,000% in 2025 compared to 2024.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: 2025 arrests for the possession of controlled substances were up 136% from 2022 totals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Pedic-lossiny arrests in 2025 were up approximately 35% from 2022.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: totals, and 2025 had the most pedic-lossing arrests since 2014 and the second most since 2006.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: These numbers make clear that New York City is moving backwards, increasingly relying on police to respond to poverty, homelessness, treatment, and behavioral health needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Every one of these arrests represent a person whose lives were further destabilized by criminal legal system involvement,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: often for conduct that poses little threat to public safety, but carries lasting consequences for employment, housing, family stability, and health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: We know firsthand from our experiences in arraignment courtrooms across this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: For instance, arraignments for cases where the most serious charges is low-level drug possession have steadily increased every year since 2021 when this data became first available.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: And are up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: over 200% citywide from 2021 to 2025, a 339% increase in the Bronx, the borough with the highest increase.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: A majority of these low-level drug offenses are ultimately dismissed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Which highlights that these are cases that should never have been prosecuted in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Prosecutions that disrupt lives, congest courtrooms, and divert resources from communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: What is more, the negative impact of increased law enforcement focused on low-level drug offenses

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: is felt most harshly in black and brown communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Almost 80% of people arraigned for low-level drug possessions are black and are Hispanic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: The city must reject calls to increase the NYPD's headcount.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: And instead invest in housing, education, youth opportunity, mental health services, and violent prevention programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: I know I'm at time, so I'm going to conclude.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You wrapping it up?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Yes, I'm wrapping up.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Ultimately, the most effective way to reduce the jail population is to reduce the number of people entering the system to begin with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: That requires ending unnecessary low-level policing, investing in communities, expanding access to community-based services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: and ensuring that support is available before a person reaches a courtroom or a jail cell.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Fisher describes DAs blatantly disregarding discovery timelines, filing certificates of compliance without obtaining NYPD materials, and argues the only real solution is fewer cases through fewer arrests, calling on the council to pressure DAs to use their existing discretion.
chapters.title: Elizabeth Fisher (Neighborhood Defender Services) on discovery noncompliance and DA accountability

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: I want to echo my colleagues'thanks to the committee for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Some of the efforts that we have heard about today will reduce the amount of time that certain individuals spend on Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: But I want to be clear, we are simply rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic without the district attorneys of this city here to take accountability for the decisions that they can make that could solve our case processing problems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: If the DAs keep requesting bail on too many New Yorkers, if they keep delaying providing discovery, if they keep delaying making plea offers that actually reflect the merits of the cases,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: then no amount of procedural efficiency is ever going to allow us to close Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The only real solution to cases moving faster is to have fewer cases to move, fewer arrests, fewer arraignments, fewer people cycling through a system that never should have touched them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And only then can our offices direct our limited resources toward the cases where legal system involvement is genuinely warranted and move those cases swiftly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: I want to turn directly to discovery because there was a lot of discussion about efforts to comply with the discovery law in the testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: But I want to talk about what is actually happening in our courtrooms and in our cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The more this state legislature chips away at the defense's right to full and speedy discovery, the slower prosecutors become about providing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: It is not too onerous.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: That is not the issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The law is not too onerous on the prosecution because when they fear the consequences of not providing discovery in a timely manner, we see that they do provide it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We have cases in Manhattan where DAs are saying on the record that they are refusing to turn over surveillance video that they have

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: three or four months into a case, despite the statutory requirements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: There is a blatant disregard for the timelines in the statute.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We are also seeing DAs file certificates compliance while simultaneously acknowledging that they haven't obtained materials central to the case from the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: I myself have handled a case recently where the prosecutor filed a certificate of compliance while failing to turn over the lead detective's entire enterprise case management system file that the NYPD testified about earlier.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Their explanation was that despite due diligence, they had been unable to obtain that case file.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The detective in that case happened to be a member of a task force embedded within the DA's office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: If what the NYPD representative here today was saying is true about the automatic access

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: that the DAs have to these files, then why are we not receiving them five, six months sometimes into a case?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The DA's often inability to obtain

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Discovery from the NYPD cannot be the reason for delaying a case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: They could solve this problem tomorrow by simply giving the DAs access to the NYPD files.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: They simply refuse to do so, and that refusal is having a human cost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Cases are stalling, people are waiting, and Rikers is staying full.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Now, I want to go back to the fundamental problem of volume.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Despite a steady decrease in major crimes, arrests are up from pre-pandemic levels, and more people are entering the system at every point.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: But prosecutions are also up, and that matters just as much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Arraignments in our office have risen steadily since 2023.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: By 2025, we were handling 15% more cases than two years prior.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And while our numbers this year are approximately the same as they were in 2023, we are facing unprecedented levels of attrition in our office due to underfunding and high caseloads.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: In June and July,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: I am losing five experienced felony attorneys.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And that might not seem like a lot, but I have an office of 30 attorneys handling caseloads.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: In two months, I'm losing one-sixth of my attorney staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: People working at our offices can no longer afford

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: to work and have families in this city at the pay that we are able to give them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And that does affect our ability to handle cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: The district attorneys have the ability to address this volume problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And the city council has the ability to demand better of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: None of this, none of the choices that they have the ability to make requires money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: None of it requires new legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: It doesn't require new ideas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: It requires them to just make different choices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: They have the tools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: They have the discretion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: What they have lacked is sufficient pressure to use those tools and use that discretion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And we are asking you to use what is within your power to apply.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: That pressure.

chapters.summary: Lee presents data showing over 1,400 bail cases with more than half dismissed, judges circumventing partially secured bond requirements, setting default 10% amounts, disapproving sureties based on income alone, and DOC removing online credit card bail options.
chapters.title: Youngmi Lee (Brooklyn Defender Services) on bail overuse and partially secured bond issues

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much for having this hearing today, which we all in New York City, all five public defender offices felt was crucial to appear and to give testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: My name is Youngmi Lee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: I'm the Director of Law and Appeals at Brooklyn Defender Services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Where I have worked for more than 28 years now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: One of my primary jobs is to assist lawyers and to also monitor our clients who are incarcerated at Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: So I just want to throw out a BDS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Kind of surprising piece of data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: In the past year, we represented more than 1,400 people who had bail set.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Most of them bail was set in criminal court arraignments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Of those 1,400 cases, more than half of them resulted in dismissals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: With close to 45 receiving ACDs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: So what does this number tell you?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: It tells us that bail is overused by judges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: I know that OCA has been making efforts and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: I was very glad to see OCA come and give city council testimony because I do believe they are making an effort to really look at the jail population at Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: At Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: But when it comes to individual judges, and I think this is citywide, probably statewide, but definitely in Brooklyn, we see judges setting very high bail on cases that shouldn't have bail set at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: We see judges circumventing that third bail form requirement that was part of the 2019 bail reforms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: That is the partially secured bond or the unsecured bond.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: In the more than 1,400 cases,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: that I reviewed, not a single one had unsecured bond.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: All of them had partially secured bond.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: The default was 10%, even though judges can do 1% to 10%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: We have also seen and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: I know Legal Aid recently did a writ on this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: We have seen judges, when a surety appears who has a work history, who has pay stubs, they go to court

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: to bail someone out on a partially secured bond, and judges will either chastise the surety for trying to bail out a loved one,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: and not follow the script that OCA has given them, but also find that they are not approved just based on their income alone,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry, based on their income and disapprove them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: These are individual cases that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: When bail can be posted, judges are even after setting unnecessary bail are now denying sureties from posting bail in the partially secured bond cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Based on our numbers, and these also include

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: a huge portion of the dismissed cases, 247 cases of those 1,400 bail cases had a much higher

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: partially secured bond amount than the commercial insurance company bond.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: We had one case where bond was set at $2,500, cash at $2,500, and partially secured bond at $10,000.
