utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: A clear indication of that particular judge

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: circumventing the partially secured bail amount that's required in all bail setting cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Over the past year, just in terms of reducing the jail population, even if it's a little bit at a time,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: DOC, and they have not come here to testify, DOC had suddenly, I want to say in the past two years, removed the credit card bail online option without explanation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: This was a method where sureties who lived out of state, who had credit cards, who had other family members could post bail online.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: That method has been completely removed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Credit card bail is still an option, but when people post bail with their credit cards at a DOC facility, which is Rikers Island right now,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: a lot of people don't realize that there is a usurious 8% fee charged, which is the fee that is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Charged also by commercial insurance company bond companies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Just to wrap up, the City Council can ask for transparency, just in terms of asking OCA to provide data on the bail amounts that are set.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: The three different forms that are required to be set when bail is posted, when partially secured bonds are disapproved by individual judges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: And finally, I just want to highlight that this is a problem citywide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Supervised release is overused by judges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: More than 50% of people who are on supervised release

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: are charged with non-qualifying offenses.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I do have some questions for the panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: I'm just going to wrap it up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: There are many cases, whether they're qualifying offenses or not, who many people who are sitting at Rikers Island who maybe should be on supervised release,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: but there are too many misdemeanor cases, too many nonviolent cases who are receiving over-programming through the supervised release program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: And I'll end it with that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks how defender offices spent discovery implementation funding and the status of data management systems. Each office describes hiring paralegals and discovery staff, investing in cloud storage and security, and notes the shared vendor was problematic.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers questions public defenders on discovery funding and data management system status

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Since the discovery reforms were passed seven years ago, your offices have received tens of millions of dollars for implementation needs.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you describe how you've spent your share of this money and what is the status of purchasing, installing, and fully implementing use of discovery data management systems?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Sure, I'll start and my colleagues might have additional information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: First and foremost, let's recognize that the discovery, initial discovery changes in the law went into effect a month before COVID hit and then Governor Cuomo suspended all criminal

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: procedure law statewide for over a year and a half.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: During that time, however, we kept pressuring the city, the state, and the district attorneys saying, we need to be prepared.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: You're not sending us discovery during the pendency of these cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: At some point, the courts are going to open up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: At some point, we are going to need to have electronic flow of discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We didn't get the millions of dollars you're talking about until three years later.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: By that time, the backlog had already been set.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: And still to today, we have homicide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: cases and other serious cases that still have to be tried based on that backlog.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: That said, you heard Mach J testify when you asked this question about the technology that each of our offices had to figure out how to stand up

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: brand new systems, just to set sort of a level set here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Before COVID, the entire city of New York's court system operated on paper only, really.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We never had electronic filing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We did during COVID.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: They have now removed that, and we're back to paper in many ways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: New York State, particularly a city like New York, was far behind the rest of the nation, and we still are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So where are we right now?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We still don't have a good vendor to provide electronic discovery monitoring systems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: It was referenced earlier today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Each of us engaged, many of us engaged in our offices with that company, and it has been a problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: That said, we also have been suggesting to the city and state that this shouldn't be an individual office's responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: We could have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: New York City has an enormous IT investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: that looks at systems all the time, and yet there's nobody building out an infrastructure that would allow us to simply not have to conduct

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: one-on-one contract negotiations with vendors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: There was a systemic way to do this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: The city and the state failed to do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So now we're picking up the pieces.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: What have we done at Legal Aid Society?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: In lieu of that, we've hired litigation assistants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So we utilized our money to bring in a brand new role at Legal Aid Society of people who are

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: trained specifically on categorizing, analyzing, reviewing, and helping attorneys prep for hearing and trial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Problem is, our budgets have been flat.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: I now have those same folks going to law school, which is a really great success story for those folks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: But I know my budget is tight and I can't rehire them right now without additional funds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: So I'm going to be behind the eight ball again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: And that's just one example.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Each of us have had to sort of go out on its own to try to figure this out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: But it has not been easy.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm just going to ask if everyone could be really tight with their responses because we do have a couple more panels after this.

chapters.summary: Skipper shares her 25 years cycling through Rikers with untreated addiction and mental illness, advocating for the Treatment Court Expansion Act, increased 6A program use, more ATI funding, and employing people with lived experience.
chapters.title: Helen Skipper (NYC Justice Peer Initiative) on lived experience and treatment court expansion

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: I'll be very brief.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: We increased our paralegal staff by 400% over the last four years, and all of that workflow for discovery reform works through the–

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Increased capacity that our paralegals have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: At BDS, we've obviously increased our discovery paralegals who receive download and then upload our discovery,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: our computer system so that we can store all of that discovery and ensure that everything is maintained for a number of years, even after a conviction or a dismissal or an acquittal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: But the computer technology to store that data, the cloud storage that's required, is costly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: But we have a system in place now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Tina Longo
utterances.text: Is it fully up and running?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: It is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Youngmi Lee
utterances.text: It is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: At Neighborhood Defender Service, we similarly have had to improve our technology and our computer systems for being able to store that discovery, but also to invest in the security around it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: There have been major concerns around attacks on our systems, on...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: Hackers in our systems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Representative
utterances.text: And so we've had to invest a lot of resources into making sure that our clients'information is safe and secure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: Similarly, at the Bronx Defenders, we have invested in discovery administrators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: We also had the challenge of engaging with the vendor who was super problematic in being able to address these unique office and borough dynamics.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: We have invested a lot in cloud storage to Young Me's BDS example as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And you pull it up and run it and it's functional.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: We are up and running.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: I would not say that it's ideal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wesley Caines
utterances.text: No, it is not.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you to the panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We're going to call up the next panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Helen Skipper.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Zachary Kastenerson, Jennifer Parrish, Chaplain Phillips, Ryan Aquanota.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yep, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I ain't got no time to read all of this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I can begin?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair and members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: My name is Helen Skip Skipper.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I serve as the Executive Director of the New York City Justice Peer Initiative, and I come before you as a directly impacted woman, a lived experience advocate, a scholervist,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: meaning a scholar and activist, and a pracademic, meaning a practitioner and academic who studies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: These systems while working inside the community to change them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I also served this city as the vice chair of the New York City Board of Correction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I offer this testimony from a place where these roles meet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Lived experience, public accountability, community expertise, and a deep belief that people closest to the problem are closest to the solution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: So we already know that the jail population has been cited at over 6,600 people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: This number changes daily, but the direction of the crisis is clear.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: If New York City is serious about closing Rikers Island and moving to a smaller borough-based jail as required by law, this population must come down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Let me say that plainly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: We do not close Rikers by managing harm more efficiently.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: close Rikers by refusing to send people to jail when they are in need of care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: We do not get to the borough-based system by polishing the machinery of detention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: We do not get anywhere in the continued criminalizations of those with behavioral health concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: And I speak this point out of experience, even though I am the vice chair of the New York City Board of Correction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: For the last three years, I need to say.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I grew up on Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I spent 25 years going in and out of Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Every time I appeared before a judge, I was suffering from a drug addiction and unchecked mental illness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: At no point did I receive support or treatment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I was always given jail.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: And because of that, I spent 25 years of my life going in and out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: The Treatment Court Expansion Act, if it had been around at that time, would have stopped that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: So I want to push for that also.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: I would like to respectfully ask that we, those of us who are in service, those of us who are pushing to do the right thing, that we push for the Treatment Care Expansion Act, which can also lower the population.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: We push for DOC to utilize the 6A program more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: And we push for more funding for alternatives to incarceration and detention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: And we employ more people with lived experience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: We have been through the fire, but we don't smell like smoke.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Helen Skipper
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm sure you know.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If you've been following our hands, we have been pushing on the 6A and a number of the things that you've mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But thank you for your advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I appreciate you.

chapters.summary: Katsenelson presents six recommendations: mandate NYPD database access for DAs, require 48-hour evidence uploads, fully staff discovery liaison units, set deadlines for data management systems, set OCME forensic testing deadlines, and require 14-day competency assessments.
chapters.title: Zachary Katsenelson (Independent Rikers Commission) on six concrete council actions to reduce jail population

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Zachary Katsenelson with the Independent Rikers Commission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Thanks for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Thank you especially for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: It's a little-known area, but with profound ramifications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Our commission has estimated that by speeding up cases just a reasonable amount, some of the goals that you mentioned, you'd have 1,400, 1,700 fewer people in Rikers on a given day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Imagine what that would mean for Rikers, for the ability to manage the population, keep people safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Imagine what that would mean for crime victims to actually get answers and accountability that much sooner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: No harm to public safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: It's just reaching the conclusion of a case sooner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: I want to talk about six things briefly where I think the council could act to help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: One is requiring the NYPD to give DAs access to their databases, make it mandatory.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Two, require NYPD officers and their supervisors to upload evidence within 48 hours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Give an explicit deadline, make sure supervisors then, maybe they have 48 hours more to approve things, move it out the door.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Three, require NYPD to fully staff the discovery liaison units.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: What we heard today is that the requests have been made and they have not been granted by the DA's offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Fourth, set a deadline by which those data management systems will actually be implemented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: We've had years of...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: No implementation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: It's time to actually set a deadline where by it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: actually happens for DAs and defenders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Set reasonable deadlines for the OCME.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: For medical examiner, they'll actually turn around forensic testing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: We've been hearing that it takes up to nine months, for instance, to get autopsy reports.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: These hold-up cases, it's not acceptable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Find out what's reasonable and get the resources there to make sure it happens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: And last, require correctional health services to...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Finish competency assessments for people that may be so mentally ill they can't stand trial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Have that happen within 14 days, 14 calendar days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: These are reasonable steps that will make a major difference.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: I'll be happy to share them directly with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Zachary Katsenelson
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I was looking for it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If you submitted it, but thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Parrish highlights that people with mental health concerns spend more time in jail, advocates for the Treatment Court Expansion Act to remove prosecutors as gatekeepers, and calls for funding mobile treatment teams, supportive housing, and interagency coordination.
chapters.title: Jennifer Parrish (Jails Action Coalition) on mental health diversion and community resource gaps

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: My name is Jennifer Parrish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: I'm a member of the Jails Action Coalition Treatment Not Jail Halt Solitary Campaign, and I work at the Urban Justice Center Mental Health Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: Thank you for convening this hearing about the court delays that contribute to over-incarceration at Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: People are suffering because of these delays, and no one's suffering more than people with mental health concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: In fact, we know that people with mental health concerns spend more time in jail than those who do not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: And we should all be ashamed that the New York City jail population overwhelmingly includes people with mental health treatment needs and those who've been diagnosed with serious mental illnesses like schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and PTSD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: We are relegating them to a place that actually exacerbates

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: their mental health needs, and we're doing this mostly pretrial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: I joined my colleagues in encouraging the council and the mayor to encourage the state government to adopt the Treatment Court Expansion Act.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: In New York City, it would speed the process for diversion by taking away the power from the prosecutors to be the gatekeepers and actually allowing a fair court system where they are arguing their position about diversion and not being the ones who control it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: But the mayor and the council have an important role in this process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: As Chief Judge Zayas said during his testimony, if we had more community resources to provide for diversion, we would be able to get people out much quicker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: And we know that there are people who are sitting on Rikers Island right now who qualify for assertive community treatment teams, intensive mobile treatment teams.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: They are not assigned because the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene will not assign them to a team because we simply do not have enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: We should be funding these programs adequately.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: We also know that they qualify for supportive housing and that people who are in jail are the least likely to be able to be placed in supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: And in fact, oftentimes people have a supportive housing application that's been approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: CHS, Correctional Health Services, is required to do these applications for people with serious mental health concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: But oftentimes the public defenders don't even realize that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: So we need a level of coordination by the mayor's office to ensure that all the agencies are working.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: working together to try to move people with serious mental illness off of Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jennifer Parrish
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Aquata describes inability to place people with dual diagnoses or violence histories in treatment programs, calls for funding mobile treatment teams, crisis respite centers, Harbor House capital funding, outpatient competency restoration pilots, and expanded ATI programs.
chapters.title: Ryan Aquata (Freedom Agenda) on treatment program gaps for people with serious mental illness

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: My name is Ryan Aquata.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: I work with Freedom Agenda, a member-led project at the Urban Justice Center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: Prior to my work at Freedom Agenda, I spent several years working in the Bronx Criminal Court for an alternative to incarceration program at the Fortune Society, working with anyone that the district attorney in court would allow me to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: We helped people find housing and jobs, get their GEDs, reunite with their families, get connected to treatment, and so much more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: Our program regularly did amazing things for the people in the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: But to be completely honest, the courts frequently did not allow me to work with most of the people that I interviewed who were detained on Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: Sometimes the DA would refuse to consider an alternative solely based on the nature of the charge, despite the evidence that ATIs are much more effective at preventing future arrests than time at Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: And given that our city budget funds the DAs, the city should be doing more to ensure that they're not creating unnecessary obstacles to diversion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: But honestly, most often the obstacle was that I couldn't piece together an adequate treatment plan for someone with serious behavioral health needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: I'd make referral after referral to programs across the five boroughs and beyond, but if someone had a dual diagnosis or a serious mental illness that wasn't currently being medicated or any kind of history of violence, each program would reject the application.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: In turn, they'd tell me that the person needed a higher level of care, a level of care that no facility seemed to offer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: So people with serious mental illness are being failed by the jails and by the court system, often even when a judge and DA are willing to release someone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: someone to a treatment program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: Because so many of our programs are at capacity, the wait lists are too long, people languish on Rikers and sink deeper into crisis, which harms everyone there and ultimately drags cases out for years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: So the city needs to ensure that we're adequately funding the programs that get people off Rikers at scale.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: That means funding enough mobile treatment teams to eliminate the long wait lists for those services, funding enough crisis respite centers to comply with local law 118 of 2023.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: It means helping Harbor House, the only residential treatment program in the five boroughs who will work with the people no one else will, get the $3 million in capital funding they need to renovate a building and bring online 60 more beds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: It means funding an outpatient competency restoration pilot so that New York City can catch up with the dozens of other states and municipalities that are already doing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: And it means funding Mach J's ATI programs and reentry services at a scale where they can both grow what's working well and also pilot new programs and initiatives that will better serve the people that existing programs don't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: And I've linked to that all of those budget demands in my written testimony and outlined several other things the council can do to address this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Aquata
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you to the panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And you'll send us the written testimony, right?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We'll call it the next panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yona Zeitz, Michelle Evans, Michael Tucker, Blake Walker.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to give another call for Chaplain Phillips.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You can begin in any order.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Just take your mic off of mute.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Turn on your mic.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yep, there you go.

chapters.summary: Evans, a formerly incarcerated journalist and software engineer, questions vendor excuses for discovery system failures, shares being denied discovery access while at Rikers, and describes being pressured into a plea deal facing 5-25 years.
chapters.title: Shel Evans on denied discovery access and vendor accountability concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: All right, there we go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Shel Evans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I'm an independent journalist, formerly incarcerated at Rikers Island and author of Rikers Island Criminalized Survivor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I am here today listening to the testimony and...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I have concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I have 20 years experience as a software engineer, and so when I hear the complaints about vendors and blaming vendors for the lack of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: sharing information and valid systems and...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I don't find that to be a valid excuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I find that to be hard to be believed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I worked for ESPN.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I worked for Time Warner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I worked for very big systems, IMD Sports Entertainment, World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I did their website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I did Tiger Woods'website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I worked on very high-scale systems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: And the fact that they are blaming vendors for not being able to share information and not being able to get

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: access to this information so that it can be handled swiftly so defendants aren't languishing on Rikers Island is a little disconcerting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I would ask that the city look at their vendor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: policy and maybe ways to bring it in-house, to develop these systems in-house.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: That was very concerning for me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: The discovery issue, I was denied discovery while I was in there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: My case languished from 2019 to 2022 when it was finally settled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: Of course, it was during the COVID pandemic, but I was denied access to discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: My lawyer requested a laptop so that I could watch the discovery that they had, videos and stuff of that nature, and I was denied that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: So that's another issue that concerns me for defendants unable to make decisions that are

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: that are informed because they're being denied discovery availability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: If you're out in the community, you can get on your computer, you can look at this discovery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: If you're locked up in Rikers, there's nothing you can do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: They deny it and you're a sitting duck.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: Also, the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: They highlighted how, what was it, like 99% or some high 90% of cases don't go to trial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: And that's due to the lengthy minimum sentences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: They scare inmates into taking pleas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I was facing 5 to 25.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: And when you're facing

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: 25 years, you stop caring about whether you're guilty or innocent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: You care about not spending 25 years for something you didn't do.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Just to ask a follow-up question, when you say that you were denied your discovery, what were the reasons that they gave?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: My lawyer didn't tell me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: She said she requested a laptop so that I could view my discovery and that they denied it, and that was all the information I was given.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so it delayed you getting, did you eventually get the discovery?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: No, never did.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You never got it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Never did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: I had to make decisions blindly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: And so that's a big concern for me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: Why, if a system wants, you know, to be fair, why would you prevent a defendant from being able to see that evidence or that discovery that has been, you know, gathered against you?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Shel Evans
utterances.text: So that was a concern.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Zaitz urges the council to hold up the budgeting process until the administration provides a measurable, funded plan to close Rikers, noting nearly 80 deaths since the 2019 closure plan and criticizing increased DOC and NYPD funding.
chapters.title: Jonas Zaitz (Cattell Center) on Rikers closure plan off track and budget demands

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Brooks-Priors, for holding today's oversight hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: My name is Jonas Zaitz, and I'm the Advocacy Director at the Cattell Center for Equity, Health, and Justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: Our members are from across the city and include people who have been formerly incarcerated and families and loved ones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And many of our members know how horrific Rikers is, and they are incredibly troubled by the closure plan being off track.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And this year's executive budget takes some steps in the right direction, and we will definitely acknowledge that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: But it ultimately falls short in making significant investments to reduce the jail population, and it leaves the closure plan in limbo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: Reducing the jail population will require decisive and large-scale action by the Mendami administration, the city council, and the courts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And the increased funding for the DOC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And the expansion of the NYPD means that the mayor is not meeting his commitments to sustainably fund, you know, investments in things that will reduce the jail population and essential services that New Yorkers rely on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And the solutions to reduce the city's jail population and advance the closure of Rikers have been thoroughly documented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And at this point, it's a matter of political will.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And the mayor and the city council can no longer continue approving the DOC budget without more details on the Burbank Jail's cost overruns and timelines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: The city clearly has no intention to meet the legal closure deadline of 2027, but has not reconfigured the plan to close.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: Rikers and provide a definitive timeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: And so we urge this committee and the full council to hold up the budgeting process until the administration puts forward a measurable and funded plan to shut down Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: The city council must use its legislative and budgetary powers to reduce the jail population, advance the closure of Rikers, and hold the mayor accountable to the closure plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: Lives are at stake, and since the city council passed the closure plan in 2019, nearly 80 people have died on Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonas Zaitz
utterances.text: Thank you for the time.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: It's not on.

chapters.summary: Tucker advocates for the Grimes-Fuller family, describing a man incarcerated pretrial for eight years with severe mental health issues denied services, and calls for subpoenaing DOC and CHS leadership and a full investigation.
chapters.title: Mike Tucker on Damian Grimes-Fuller's 8-year pretrial incarceration and mental health denial

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Good afternoon, and thank you, Chairperson Brooks-Powers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: My name is Mike Tucker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: I'm here representing the Grimes-Fuller family.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: You can't say criminal justice reform and looking into all of these agencies without talking about Damian Grimes-Fuller.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Inmate at Records Island, booking case number 113-220-0739.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: This man has been incarcerated in a pretrial setting for eight years, 2,920 days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: I went and spoke on June 10th at the city executive budget hearing, and I asked that y'all use your subpoena power to subpoena the Department of Corrections head as well as CHS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: These organizations are getting millions of dollars.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: This man has a severe mental health issue, and he has been denied access.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Only reason that this case has been looked into is because I reached out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Starting with the public advocate's office, as well as Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Zyce, that's sitting right next to me, the mayor's office, anybody that I can get to look into this man's case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: It doesn't have any reason why a person should be set in pretrial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: hearing settings with mental health issues and being denied services, his rights of being violated, and they're asking for more money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: What are you doing with the money that you already have?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Why haven't this man been taken to trial?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: This extends to the DA's office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: The legal aid society as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: You got 18B lawyers that are representing this man that are not speaking up for this man.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: I'm here pleading with you and begging you to please look into this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Ask for a full investigation because questions needs to be answered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mike Tucker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm just going to ask a member from the committee staff to just capture the information of the gentleman you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: This, oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We were waiting for you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public
utterances.text: There you go.

chapters.summary: Phillips shares two decades of Rikers experience, describes officers writing detainees as refusals for court, highlights DOC's failure to implement Local Law 42, and details deaths of people with mental health concerns due to lack of care.
chapters.title: Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips on Rikers mental health failures and deaths

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Peace and blessings, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: I'm Chaplain Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Victoria Phillips, or everyone calls me Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: V.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: I'm the CEO and founder of Visionary V Ministries and the co-founder of the Jails Action Coalition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: I would like to say, Jack, the Jails Action Coalition, we actually formed in 2011 because of the barbaric conditions on Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Over the last two decades, I've worked in Rikers in the capacities of nursing, cognitive behavioral therapy, monitoring those with serious mental illness, and some forms of chaplaincy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: I say that because all those areas have afforded me different access to the island in different ways.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: So I've been able to see far things greater than anyone in a civilian manner probably would.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: There are a lot of things I've testified on the record about for the last 15 years regarding Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And so I just want to highlight a few of them that goes along with the testimonies that was said today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: It was mentioned today that, you know,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: There's reasons why people aren't produced for court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And as I was sitting there listening, it reminded me of a time several years ago when I testified before the city council and raised the concerns of officers who directly told a detainee in front of me,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: well, we're going to write you up as a refusal after he was so upset that no one came to get him for court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And the author.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: that was in the unit was just like, well, what you want me to do about it?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: We'll write you up as a refusal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And I want to remind this council that those things very much do occur and that it should not be ignored because the truth comes from someone formerly incarcerated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: So I want to remind this council of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: I also want to remind this council that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: You know, it was just released and said that DOC would actually be starting a pilot for implementing Local Law 42 in the fall or for December.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Can I get a few more?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Anything you don't get to, you could put in writing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: In writing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: But there's no dates for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And I want to highlight that, and I'll finish by highlighting this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: DOC continues to lie.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And I want you to really understand what that means for someone in custody who does not get care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: 68% of those in custody right now with a mental health concern will not get adequate mental health treatment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And I want to put names on, like Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Carter, because Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Carter is someone who cycled throughout all of our systems that this city is responsible for.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Adequately giving funding for, and they failed him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And then he ended up in Rikers Island and died within less than 72 hours of being there when he should have never been there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And over the last two decades, I've seen countless people that should not be there decompensate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: I've seen wardens reach out to me because they do not know what to do with them or where to put them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And I want to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: want to just, you know, I'm the one who reaches out to parents and loved ones and sisters and brothers and help them through their grief and funeral arrangements and everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Every time somebody dies, four people died this year, three of them were around mental health concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Last year in 2025, the rates of deaths were three times that what they were in 2024.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And most of those deaths were around medical access or care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And so I'll just close by saying we cannot just be happy to have a mayor of hope and we're still not bringing true change.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And when we say that money needs to be allocated into the community, the community is what prevents people from coming into the system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And if the resources are not there, we're still failing ourselves on domestic soil.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: And that is not acceptable for me with a mother buried in a military cemetery.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chaplain Dr. Victoria Phillips
utterances.text: Have a blessed day.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers checks for any remaining in-person or online testimony, finds none, and closes the hearing, thanking everyone for their time and contributions.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers closes hearing after checking for remaining testimony

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you to this panel.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You can see if anybody in the room wishes to...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If there's anyone in the room that wish to testify, we ask that you see the sergeant.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm seeing none online, but I also want to check the room to see if Hyukyung Kwan or Ramona Fariva is here.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Not hearing anything.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay, so with that, we are going to close out this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I do thank everyone for their time and their contribution to such an important conversation today.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

summary: Oversight hearing by the NYC Council Committee on Criminal Justice on improving court operations to reduce the jail population, held June 25, 2026.
Chief Administrative Judge Joseph Zayas testified on scheduling orders, discovery compliance data, problem-solving courts, and the need for stakeholder cooperation to reduce case delays.

Public defender organizations, MOCJ, NYPD, and community advocates testified on discovery delays, bail practices, mental health services, and the need for greater investment in alternatives to incarceration.
title: Committee on Criminal Justice
